GENERAL SERVICES ADMINISTRATION

Federal Acquisition Service

Assisted Acquisition Services Division

Southeast Sunbelt Region

Performance Work Statement (PWS)

CUI/SP-Procure

Section 1 – BACKGROUND

CDC is working to develop a future state public health ecosystem where data flows and information systems are coordinated and interoperable across healthcare and public health at all levels of government. The North Star ecosystem model, often referred to as the North Star Architecture (NSA), describes the who, what, and how of a future state public health ecosystem. This ecosystem model is part of CDC’s Data Modernization Initiative (DMI) of building the right foundation and accelerating data to action. It aims to support our public health partners in translating the vision of data modernization into sustainable solutions that allow more focus on public health action and less on data management while preserving state, local, territorial, and tribal (STLT) control of data and decision-making autonomy. Many improvements are needed and will enable more robust data access, analysis, and use for decision making.

Multiple components will be necessary for the North Star Architecture. CDC has defined several “building blocks” that need to be developed at the agency in order to support the NSA vision. One of these foundational building blocks is Enterprise Data Exchange, commonly referred to as “DEX.”  The DEX building block will centralize data ingestion, enterprise-grade validation (where possible or required), and provisioning of data to CDC programs, all while providing visibility and observability to customers in the data lifecycle at CDC. The agency is moving away from legacy ingestion systems and mechanisms, and bespoke endpoints, in order to reduce the burden on those customers that send data to the CDC. The agency is moving towards supporting modern, machine-to-machine, automated exchange of data. The agency is centralizing validation services in order to reduce redundant IT spend on bespoke, and mostly, on-premises, processing systems within the agency. The agency will be depositing all data it receives into CDC’s internal data lake, commonly referred to as “EDAV”. From here, the programs will access, process, transform, and create valuable public health insights which will drive public health guidance, policy, and outcomes.

Section 2 – PURPOSE

Section 2 – PURPOSE

The purpose of this task order is to support the development, maintenance, and continued modernization of the product called Enterprise Data Exchange (DEX). DEX is intended to be a platform that includes data ingestion, validation, routing, streaming, provisioning, and a portal(s) and service that not only meets but exceeds customer expectations. DEX will receive data from hospitals, clinics, laboratories, intermediaries (e.g., Report Stream, APHL’s AIMS platform) non-profits, other government agencies, and state, local, territorial, and tribal health departments (STLTs). DEX is expected to provide data and other metadata back to these same entities through APIs and portals. DEX will have to parse hundreds of data streams, route, process, and provision. DEX will be expected to interoperate with intermediaries, collaborate, and even share code where possible. DEX will be closely aligned with CDC/ONC joint initiatives, such as TEFCA, ONC FAST, ETOR, etc. This platform must also provide standards testing and validation (content and structure) for new and upcoming standards to align with the public health ecosystem.

Figure 1. DEX Conceptual Architecture

Using Figure 1 above, this task order will focus on the following functions or activities:  Supporting the Data Producers and Data Consumers, maintaining backend services for auth (where required), DEX transport (e.g., APIs, portal), DEX observability (e.g., APIs, portal), DEX validation (FHIR), and getting that data with its associated metadata into CDC’s Enterprise Data and Analytics Visualization (EDAV) data lake for use by programs. It may include some routing and working with existing transport (legacy) systems for getting data into CDC (where required). 

DEX is employing modular contracting. This SOO is focused on Customer Experience, which includes DEX Transport and DEX Observability. DEX Validation for HL7v2, CDA, CSV, and XML formats are covered under a separate task order. The separate task order also covers shared services that are required for all the validation pipelines, such as streaming and routing. The Contractor who is awarded the Customer Experience task order will be expected to interoperate and cooperate with the DEX Validation contractor.

Section 3 – SCOPE OF WORK

Enable and utilize modern authentication and authorization technology and tools

Provide easy, efficient onboarding processes and documentation for data senders and new data streams

Provide customer support during their onboarding and use of DEX’s APIs and existing data feeds that have been migrated to use DEX observability. 

Note that the CDC does not have an estimate on the number of customers. Customers may include external customers from state, local, tribal health departments, state public health laboratories, private laboratories, hospitals, hospital systems, HIEs (electronic health information exchanges), and intermediaries such as APHL AIMS and Report Stream.  Internal users are all programs at CDC. The expectation is that we will serve thousands of senders concurrently, and some are high volume senders.

Transition senders off proprietary technologies (e.g., PHINMS, etc.) and CDC on-prem solutions

Ensure data is available to CDC programs within minutes of arrival

WS3. Infrastructure and automated deployment

Goal/Vision: Deliver customer features faster. 

Top Problems to Solve:

Reduce upgrade burden

Same day, automated deployments of capabilities, features, or bug-fixes

Specific features of DEX can be deployed without having to re-deploy the entire codebase

Ensure a large, complex, cloud-based, cloud-native, operational infrastructure is maintainable.

Ensure APIs can handle large amounts of data or pandemic-level data flows without performance degradation

Based on the Government’s current understanding of the problem space, the following are notional objectives for the first 6 months of performance. Note: These notional objectives are intended to help focus the contractor’s attention at the outset of the project. The discovery/user research activities will shape the objectives.

A product plan and backlog for each workstream, including but not limited to the DEX portal(s), DevSecOps, and all DEX APIs

Production Upload API in use by at least 8 distinct CDC customers.

MVP Processing Status API and Transport Observability API user tested with at least 3 distinct existing CDC data streams. 

MVP DEX portal(s) with user testing on at least 6 distinct data senders and 6 distinct CDC program.

Documentation, developer examples, and onboarding instructions for use of the APIs has been created and made available to the public.

CI/CD for all application and infrastructure code (infrastructure-as-code with Terraform).

Section 4 – TASKS TO BE PERFORMED

Section 3 – SCOPE OF WORK

The scope of this effort requires the contractor to employ human-centered design (HCD) and service design techniques, product management methods, and agile software development principles to build a modern Enterprise Data Exchange (DEX) that will meet the needs and goals of stakeholders who send data to the CDC and those CDC programs who receive data from external sources. The scope of this task order includes activities that can categorized as: building and supporting operations, infrastructure, IaC, DevSecOps practices, APIs, developer and customer documentation, providing onboarding and customer support, product development and support, architecture, software development and support, software testing, user experience research, design and development, service design and development, content design and development, and all portal and website development.

The Government envisions the DEX modernization under this PWS to be broken out into several workstreams (Section 3), each of which encompasses several tasks (Section 4) to be executed by Product Teams (Section 5).

WS1. Data Observability

Goal/Vision: Provide visibility and observability of data flowing through DEX to all customers

Top Problems to Solve:

Establish a UX that supports efficient onboarding to DEX and use of APIs

Establish a UX that supports the visibility of data as it flows through the DEX pipelines and processes (e.g., ingestion, validation, and provisioning)

Establish customer-facing portal(s) for human-readability of data and metadata by DEX customers. Customers include both CDC-internal programs and CDC-external data exchange partners (e.g., STLTs) 

Establish a DEX-internal portal for visibility into functioning of the entire DEX ecosystem, health of pipelines and services

Provide a processing status API that backs customer and DEX-internal portal(s)

Provide customers support for CDC data senders (e.g., STLT) and CDC data consumers (e.g., CDC Programs) in using the DEX and DEX observability components

Establish a web presence for DEX on www.cdc.gov

WS2. Data Transport

Goal/Vision: Reduce the burden on those who send data to the CDC, by reducing their operational complexity

Top Problems to Solve:

Enable both person and machine-to-machine data exchange to the agency via DEX-specific transport

Integrating with existing CDC data feeds, migration to DEX transport and/or observability of the existing transport

Enable and utilize modern authentication and authorization technology and tools

Provide easy, efficient onboarding processes and documentation for data senders and new data streams

Provide customer support during their onboarding and use of DEX’s APIs and existing data feeds that have been migrated to use DEX observability.
agile_methodology

Section 4 – TASKS TO BE PERFORMED

The Government expects that the contract Technical Product Owners will serve as the voice of their end-users, which is to say they are member primarily responsible for maximizing the value delivered by the team by ensuring that the team backlog is aligned with customer and stakeholder needs. regularly update their product roadmaps, resolve prioritize within their workstreams and communicate impacts. The CDC DEX Platform Owner will set the overall direction of the DEX Platform and resolve conflicts in joint priorities between workstreams. There is one Platform Owner who is responsible for all DEX products and services.  DEX is envisioned to have between eight and ten products across the entire DEX platform when at full maturity and we expect that the platform will have multiple technical product owners, each associated with at least one DEX product.  

As is customary in agile development, priorities may shift over time. These shifting priorities will be accounted for through collaboration between the Government (Product Owner, CDC stakeholders, etc.) and the contractor to establish a shared understanding of the work, which will be planned for and communicated through standard agile practices such as product road mapping, backlog prioritization and sprint planning. For example, the CDC may decide during performance that additional functionality may need to be added to the roadmap/backlog, at which time the Government would work with the contractor to plan for and prioritize those tasks, as relevant.

All software code developed will live in one of DEX’s open-source repositories inside CDC’s CDCgov org space. Infrastructure code is hosted in a private repo inside CDC’s cdcent org space.

4.4. Transition In/Out and Retrospective

The contractor shall work in conjunction with the Product Owner and the CDC team, as well as either the existing contractor development team (when transitioning onto the project) or the onboarding contractor development team (when transitioning off the project) to facilitate transition in/out activities. The contractor shall build in knowledge and document-sharing tasks into the normal sprint cadence in order to efficiently manage the transitioning on or off of the project. As a baseline, these activities should take place over 2 sprint cycles (4 weeks) at a minimum, although more may be needed.

The contractor shall:

Map out transition in/out tasks to be incorporated over at least a 2-sprint schedule with the CDC team and/or the onboarding contractor

Ensure that all relevant project-specific knowledge and documentation is either shared or received before any existing development team rolls off the project

Address any knowledge or documentation gaps that could inhibit the contractor’s ability to effectively perform the tasks/roles as outlined above in sections 1 and 2

Develop playbooks and developer how-to documentation throughout the entire life of the contract that can be passed on to new/replacement development teams to ease transitioning in/out. The contractor may either update existing documentation or create original documentation, but any documentation will ultimately be owned and managed by the Government.

Ensure that the concept of “ownership” of the product, which is to mean the successful transition of true responsibility for the care and feeding of the system, is transferred from the outgoing contractor to the incoming contractor(s) both at the beginning of the task order and at the end. This would generally be finalized during the 2nd sprint of the transition period.

Conduct a project retrospective activity with the Product Owner and CDC team that analyzes data gathered during performance around goals, timeline, major events, and success or failures

Update the product roadmap for continued scaling of the product through continuous design and agile processes

Contractor Personnel

Ensure that appropriate configuration control and change management processes are aligned with CDC policy and standards

The contractor shall:

Iteratively design solutions toward the end user experience so that products meet end user needs and goals, including, contributing to the development of the product vision, product roadmap(s), and product backlog(s) with associated user stories

Prototype and test design concepts with end users

Incorporate UI/UX principles that ensures that diverse groups of users are represented throughout the development process. This should cut across a variety of socioeconomic factors such as income, race/ethnicity, education level, geographic location, etc. Our goal is to ensure that user research captures feedback from a broad set of stakeholders of public health services and is not focused on a narrow subset of the population.

4.2	Continuous Agile Development, DevSecOps, DataOps, and Product Management

Working in conjunction with the federal Product Owner and relevant CDC stakeholders, the contractor shall utilize agile and DevSecOps methodologies to develop and deploy features related to the approaches proposed during design/discovery, as well as to support the continuous development of DEX. This includes appropriately maintaining and refreshing all existing features and functionality, as well as supporting the continued integration of new data sources and streams, as relevant.

The contractor shall:

All software code developed will live in one of DEX’s open-source repositories inside CDC’s CDCgov org space. Infrastructure code is hosted in a private repo inside CDC’s cdcent org space.

The Contractor will use agile development principles, with necessary documentation, human-centered design, and an extensible infrastructure. The Government expects that the development process will be collaborative and iterative, using the twelve principles established by the Agile Manifesto as well as the Digital Services Playbook. Usability testing and other user research methods must be conducted at regular intervals through the development process (not just at the beginning or end), with all electronic artifacts from usability testing and/or other research methods with end-users being made available at the end of every applicable sprint. The principles outlined in UX Contract Principles on the TechFARHub must be integrated through the development lifecycle of any application, system, or features that are created as a result of this work. All features should be designed to be easily hosted in a cloud-based environment, and any code developed will live in an open-source repository in CDC’s GitHub.

4.1	Discovery and Design

Working in conjunction with the federal Product Owner and the CDC team, the contractor shall conduct discovery work around the needs of DEX users. DEX external customers include those who send data to and receive data from the CDC. They will include, but are not limited to, hospitals, clinics, laboratories, STLTs, intermediaries (e.g., APHL AIMS platform, Report Stream), other government agencies, and other entities. DEX internal customers include different groups of CDC stakeholders, who will utilize the data that DEX ingests, validates, and provisions. These customers use this data to conduct national disease surveillance, track immunizations/vaccinations, identify emerging public health challenges, and guide policy decisions at the national level. DEX must have observability, visibility, and transparency for its customers. DEX products must be consumable by and meet the needs of its customers.   The contractor shall utilize HCD techniques to design approaches to meeting these needs in the most impactful manner possible.

The contractor shall:


Review user research conducted prior to the beginning of this contract (e.g., research completed by USDS-led initiatives already in progress at CDC, and other sources)

Conduct research into relevant STLT, clinics, laboratories, CDC, and other health organizations, processes, systems, and policies to understand their needs and goals

4.3	Product Support

The contractor shall provide technical product support to internal and external DEX users, by answering user questions, explaining features, clarifying operating instructions, providing troubleshooting tips and providing training to new and existing users. Additionally, the contractor shall maintain product documentation to assist users in finding answers to questions. When necessary, the contractor shall escalate issues to the appropriate internal support.

The contractor shall:

Answer support questions via email messaging tools and chat tools

Train existing and new DEX users on product enablement to improve overall customer experience on an ad hoc basis to help supplement the product documentation created to assist users

Work directly with customers to provide product and technical product support, understand customer needs, and find solutions to common problems

Work with internal teams to escalate issues, share customer feedback and make recommendations on potential product improvements

Contribute to improving consumer-facing documentation

This will be the first path of escalation to the product teams (e.g., ingestion, validation, and release automation team). The contractor shall solve common technical issues and work on scripts and automation to improve their workflow. This may include assisting external customers with onboarding to APIs, troubleshooting issues with DEX APIs, and onboarding or access issues in the internal CDC environment. When necessary, the contractor shall escalate issues to the appropriate internal team.

Section 5 – ORDER LEVEL MATERIALS (OLM)/OTHER DIRECT COSTS (ODC)

The Government may require the Contractor to purchase cloud space, hardware, software, equipment, tools, related supplies and materials that are integral and necessary for the services being required under this task order. Such items shall be incidental to and associated with the overall functions being performed through this task order. ODC’s necessary for the performance of this task order shall be reimbursed in accordance with the billing and payment clauses of this contract. Prior approval from the Contracting Officer’s Representative is required before purchases are made. All OLM’s/ODC’s are considered the sole property of CDC and proprietary rights are reserved.

Note: At the time of writing of this task order, all development work for this task order is expected to take place inside CDC’s own cloud environment. The cost for this environment is covered through other funding mechanisms and is not the responsibility of the contractor.


Travel

Some travel will be necessary for the performance of this task order. CDC’s Data Exchange (DEX) has quarterly onsite meetings and there is an expectation that some contracting staff will attend those meetings in-person. The Contracting Officer’s Representative (COR) will work with the contractor to identify those individuals who shall attend. In addition, there is an expectation that DEX government employees and contractors alike will participate in FHIR Connect-a-thons, attend conferences, give presentations, and generally participate in the technology-related public health community. Again, the Contracting Officer’s Representative (COR) will work with the contractor to identify those individuals who shall participate and attend at these type events.

The cost of travel and any “other related items” is considered “Materials” (see FAR Clause 52.232-7). All travel shall be performed in accordance with Federal Travel Regulations and/or the Joint Travel Regulation. The contractor will be required to submit evidence supporting any claim for travel related expenses. Section 6 – ADMINISTRATIVE REQUIREMENTS AND SCHEDULE OF DELIVERABLES

     6.1	Kick-Off Meeting

The Contractor shall attend a kick-off meeting between the Contractor’s project manager, CDC Contracting Officer, and CDC COR virtually to discuss expectations for performance and set initial priorities for the work within 10 days after award or option period effective date, if an option is exercised. For the option period, teleconference is sufficient for participants.

         Monthly Reporting

The Contractor shall provide a written monthly report, broken out by each task, containing, at a minimum, the following information:

A brief description of tasks completed during the reporting period and significant events regarding the project;

Sufficient information for confirmation of effort covered by the invoices for the same period, workload planning, and evaluation of task performance;

The report is due to the CDC Contracting Officer’s Representative (COR) electronically (MS Word or equivalent) no later than the 10th calendar day of the month. Late delivery will delay invoice review and approval.

6.3   Quality Control Plan

a.	The Contractor shall ensure all work will be performed in accordance with the contract requirements, in compliance with the FAR 52.212-4. The contractor shall maintain and submit to the Government within 10 days after award, a complete Quality Control Plan (QCP) addressing the inspection system used to ensure the requirements of this contract are met. The contracting officer shall notify the contractor of acceptance or any required modifications.  The QCP shall include the following minimum requirements:

A description of the inspection system to cover all major services and deliverables.  The description shall include specifics as to the areas to be inspected on both a scheduled and unscheduled basis, frequency of inspections, and the title of inspectors.

A description of the methods to be used for identifying and preventing defects in the quality of service performed.

A description of the records to be kept to document inspections and corrective or preventative actions taken.

All records of inspections performed shall be retained and made available to the Government upon request throughout the contract performance period, and for the period after contract completion, until final settlement of any claims under this contract.

Work Expectations

The contractor shall work in partnership and collaborate successfully with other contractor(s) who will also be working on DEX.  DEX utilizes modular contracting to serve its needs. The work will be highly collaborative, will necessitate working on integrated teams or even across teams, and with a focus on the overall benefit to the DEX product. The contractor should provide opportunities for all team members to participate in roles such as on-call production, architectural review, and utility/automation tool development. The team should carefully work to avoid slowdowns in project velocity.

The team should apply design thinking techniques (user research, analytics, etc.) to understand who our stakeholders are and what they need and when they are getting it. Use that knowledge to design, build, operate, maintain, and evolve services that seek to maximize the success and impact of DEX.

4.5 Optional Teams and Tasks

Due to the nature of the services described in this Performance Work Statement, the Government may need additional support during the life of this contract. These optional tasks could provide DEX with additional resources needed to support an outbreak or activation of CDC’s Emergency Operations Center (EOC), add additional APIs or portals to DEX’s portfolio based on discovery activities that have yet to take place, or to add additional resources to support the growth and maturity of the DEX product (e.g., an increase in the number of senders/receivers that DEX is supporting). However, as priorities change over time, team composition and focus of task areas may change.

TASK ORDER CLAUSES AND PROVISIONS

All applicable contract clauses, provisions, and terms and conditions from the PRIME BPA/GSA Multiple Award Schedule (MAS) Information Technology Schedule are hereby incorporated into this task order. 

52.217-8 Option to Extend Services. Option to Extend Services (Nov 1999)

The Government may require continued performance of any services within the limits and at the rates specified in the contract. These rates may be adjusted only as a result of revisions to prevailing labor rates provided by the Secretary of Labor. The option provision may be exercised more than once, but the total extension of performance hereunder shall not exceed 6 months. The Contracting Officer may exercise the option by written notice to the Contractor within __30 days___ 

(End of clause)

FAR 52.217-9 Option to Extend the Term of the Contract. (Mar 2000)

(a) 	The Government may extend the term of this contract by written notice to the Contractor within 1 day provided that the Government gives the Contractor a preliminary written notice of its intent to extend at least 30 days before the contract expires. The preliminary notice does not commit the Government to an extension.

(b) 	If the Government exercises this option, the extended contract shall be considered to include this option clause.

(c) 	The total duration of this contract, including the exercise of any options under this clause, shall not exceed 3.5 years.

(End of clause)

FAR 52.204-27 Prohibition on a ByteDance Covered Application.

As prescribed in 4.2203(c), insert the following clause:

Prohibition on a ByteDance Covered Application (Jun 2023)

(a) Definitions. As used in this clause—

Covered application means the social networking service TikTok or any successor application or service developed or provided by ByteDance Limited or an entity owned by ByteDance Limited.

Information technology, as defined in 40 U.S.C. 11101(6)—

(1) Means any equipment or interconnected system or subsystem of equipment, used in the automatic acquisition, storage, analysis, evaluation, manipulation, management, movement, control, display, switching, interchange, transmission, or reception of data or information by the executive agency, if the equipment is used by the executive agency directly or is used by a contractor under a contract with the executive agency that requires the use—

(i) Of that equipment; or

(ii) Of that equipment to a significant extent in the performance of a service or the furnishing of a product;

If the offeror requests an oral feedback meeting, the offeror should provide the following information:

●	Primary point of contact;

●	List of participants with titles (e.g., Senior Vice President);

●	List of topics to assist GSA better prepare for the oral feedback meeting; and,

●	Preference for in-person, telephone, or web-based conferencing (if available).

The contracting officer will make every effort to schedule the oral feedback meeting within five business days of the offeror’s receipt of the notification of award.  

B.3.	Component 3 - Oral Feedback Meeting

During the oral feedback meeting, GSA’s objectives are to provide:

●	Reasonable responses to written questions submitted by the offeror;

●	Cohesive explanations for the evaluation conclusions and contract award decisions;

●	Any additional information about the fairness and impartiality of the evaluation and whether the award decision was rational;

●	Reasonable responses to additional questions raised during the meeting; 

●	Additional transparency into the underlying competition process; and,

●	A greater understanding of the evaluation and award process.

GSA will not provide any information that is prohibited by law or regulation.

B.4.	Component 4 - Post Oral Feedback Meeting Questions

Within two business days after the conclusion of the oral feedback meeting, the offeror may submit a list of written questions to the contracting officer.  The contracting officer will provide a written response within five business days and, unless otherwise noted, the INFORM process is concluded.

If the offeror elects not to submit a list of written questions within two business days after the oral feedback meeting, the INFORM process is concluded.

GSAM 552.238-115 Special Ordering Procedures for the Acquisition of Order-Level Materials.

As prescribed in 538.7204(b), insert the following clause:

Special Ordering Procedures for the Acquisition of Order-Level Materials (APR 2022)

(a)  Definition.

Order-level materials, as used in this clause supplies and/or services acquired in direct support of an individual task or delivery order placed against a Federal Supply Schedule (FSS) contract or FSS blanket purchase agreement (BPA), when the supplies and/or services are not known at the time of Schedule contract or FSS BPA award. The prices of order-level materials are not established in the FSS contract or FSS BPA. Order-level materials acquired following the procedures in paragraph (d) of this clause are done so under the authority of the FSS program, pursuant to 41 U.S.C. 152(3), and are not open market items, which are discussed in FAR 8.402(f).

SECTION C – DESCRIPTION/SPECIFICATION/ 

PERFORMANCE WORK STATEMENT (PWS)


EpiSync Product Development and Operations

Section 1 – Background

Background on CDC’s Public Health Data Strategy (PHDS)

CDC's Public Health Data Strategy (PHDS) outlines the data, technology, policy, and administrative actions essential to exchange critical core data efficiently and securely across healthcare and public health. 

The strategy is designed to describe a path to address gaps in public health data, helping the nation become response-ready, promote health equity, and improve health outcomes for all.

To advance core public health missions, CDC’s Public Health Data Strategy addresses the CDC imperative to consistently deliver public health information and guidance to Americans in near real-time. 

The Public Health Data Strategy outlines four Public Health Data Goals:

Strengthen the core of public health data

Accelerate access to analytic and automated solutions to support public health investigations and advance health equity 

Visualize and share insights to inform public health action 

Advance more open and interoperable public health data 

To create accountability for these Public Health Data Goals, CDC has created the new Office of Public Health Data, Surveillance, and Technology (OPHDST), which will lead and strengthen engagement and collaboration with jurisdictions, CDC programs, and private partners, and focus the execution of data modernization activities.

Full details on the Public Health Data Strategy can be found here:  https://www.cdc.gov/ophdst/public-health-data-strategy/public_health_data_strategy-final-p.pdf

See also

https://www.cdc.gov/about/pdf/advisory/dsw-recommendations-report.pdf

https://www.cdc.gov/nndss/case-surveillance-modernization/index.html

Background on Case Surveillance

The PHDS identifies six core data sources: case surveillance data, laboratory-based diagnostic testing data, syndromic surveillance/emergency department data, immunization/vaccine administration data, hospital capacity data, and death data/vital statistics.

The EpiSync project will focus on only one of the core data sources: case surveillance data.

Case surveillance is foundational to public health practice. It helps public health officials understand diseases and their spread and determine appropriate actions to reduce disease spread.

The EpiSync project will focus on only one of the core data sources: case surveillance data.

Case surveillance is foundational to public health practice. It helps public health officials understand diseases and their spread and determine appropriate actions to reduce disease spread. 

Case surveillance starts at state, tribal, local, and territorial public health departments (STLTs).  The STLTs work with healthcare facilities to get the information needed to monitor, control, and prevent these reportable diseases and conditions in their communities. Healthcare facilities are legally mandated to report a set of conditions to their STLT public health department(s) – this is called “case reporting.”  STLTs also notify CDC about certain conditions so we can track them for the whole country – this is called “case notification.” Through the National Notifiable Diseases Surveillance System (NNDSS), CDC monitors about 120 notifiable diseases and receives data on additional conditions at jurisdiction’s discretion.  

Case surveillance by STLT public health officials captures information used to understand where diseases are occurring, how people are exposed to the diseases, and which groups are most heavily impacted. The information captured includes a set of data elements that defines information to be collected about:

Who is affected? —the demographic, clinical, and epidemiologic characteristics

Where are they affected? —the geographic distribution of disease

When is it happening?

How are they affected? —the course of clinical illness and care received

More information about public health case surveillance can be found here:  https://www.cdc.gov/nndss/about/index.html

To answer the above questions, STLT health departments collect a set of data elements, based on condition, that are needed for their public health case investigation and local disease control efforts. A subset of those data elements is then sent to CDC in the case notification. Currently there is no universal machine-readable format for defining such sets of data elements, even though this concept recurs in many guises.  For example, CDC’s Message Mapping Guides (MMGs) describe a set of case elements for case notification of a group of conditions, but MMGs are tightly tied to one data format (HL7 version 2) and are not used in a machine-readable form by STLTs.  As a second example within the STLT’s information system, the NBS PageBuilder Templates play a nearly identical role as MMGs, but users must manually translate MMGs into PageBuilder Templates, and those templates are only machine-readable by NBS, so they are not universal.

More information about MMGs can be found here:  https://www.cdc.gov/nndss/trc/mmg/index.html. 

The Current MMG, Investigation, and Case Notification Roundtrip Flow

Currently when a new notifiable condition arises, or when CDC epidemiologists require new information about an existing condition, CDC may create or update an MMG for use by the STLTs.  STLTs will then manually adopt it as part of their Case Investigation workflows and will eventually begin to send Case Notifications to the CDC that meet those new/updated MMGs.

This can be thought of as a round-trip data flow:  MMGs flow from the CDC to the STLTs, and Case Notifications that meet those definitions flow back from the STLTs to the CDC.

At least that’s what’s supposed to happen. In practice this process is laborious and can take months or years and is further hampered by STLTs’ use of a profusion of other legacy data formats and routing mechanisms, that are not compatible with MMGs.  During a health emergency, the current workflow may hamper public health’s ability to be response-ready.

Section 2 – Purpose of EpiSync – Fixing the Roundtrip Case Data Flow

The EpiSync project will further the goals of the Public Health Data Strategy by mitigating, improving, or eliminating slow steps in this flow, while also improving data quality and completeness. EpiSync will place a special emphasis on efficiency, response-readiness, and near-real-time updates within PHDS Goal 1:  Strengthen the core of public health data.

Definition of a Universal Question Template

A key part of fixing the roundtrip case data flow problem requires automating the outbound flow of requested information from the CDC.  We therefore propose a new concept, the universal Question Template (QuTe), defined to be a machine-readable specification of elements to be captured for a particular condition.  The Question Template can differ from condition to condition, and can differ over time, and STLTs could generate their own local Question Templates.  In some cases, many conditions can reuse the same Question Template.  As mentioned above, currently there is no such thing as a universal, machine-readable Question Template for public health case investigations, even though this concept recurs already in many guises.  

Therefore, a key part of the EpiSync project will be to construct a specification for Question Templates, and wrap software tools around them, that can be used by both STLTs and the CDC.

The Proposed New Question Template, Investigation, and Case Notification Roundtrip Flow

Therefore, a key part of the EpiSync project will be to construct a specification for Question Templates, and wrap software tools around them, that can be used by both STLTs and the CDC.

The Proposed New Question Template, Investigation, and Case Notification Roundtrip Flow

How might this work?   When a new notifiable condition arises, or when epidemiologists require new information about an existing condition, CDC may create or update a Question Template for use by the STLTs.  It could automatically propagate into STLT Case Investigation workflows – likely still with some human oversight, but not with slow manually copying data elements.   STLTs can then immediately adopt it as part of their Case Investigation workflows and immediately begin to send valid Case Notifications to the CDC that meets those new/updated Question Template specs.

Section 3 – Goals and Top Problems to Solve

EpiSync Goals

EpiSync is part of a multi-year CDC effort to improve this Question Template and Notification process and services. At this time, our overall long-term goals include:

Situational Awareness – Enabling Case Notifications to synchronize from STLTs to the CDC in near real-time. Further, enabling the CDC to process those Case Notifications and make them useful to CDC Programs, Response Teams, and STLTs in near real-time.

Flexibility – Enabling a common machine-readable Question Template language for defining the set of questions and elements associated with a notifiable disease/condition.   Enabling Question Templates to be pushed in near real-time to STLTs and others, so that Case Investigation, Case Notification, routing, and validation tools can be quickly updated in a crisis.

Scalable Capacity – Enabling cloud-elastic autoscaling to meet massive case volumes, such as during a pandemic. 

Faster and less burdensome data – Enabling both STLTs and the CDC to create and use Question Templates.   Reducing burden of mapping of STLT Question Templates to CDC Question Templates, through use of mapping tools and standardized machine-readable element specifications. Allow for non-PII STLT-defined elements to be included in Case Notifications to the CDC. 

Synchronizing Data – Automatically synchronizing case data from STLT source-of-truth systems to decrease and then eliminate the amount of time spent on the current reconciliation process. (Currently STLTs must regularly perform manual reconciliation analyses to ensure CDC and STLT data corroborate).

The goals and “top problems to solve” listed above are subject to change based on the natural evolution of the Government’s and contractor’s understanding of the problem space through continued discovery, user research, and partner engagement. The prioritization of the problems to solve will be managed by the EpiSync product owner at the CDC and achieved through a regularly updated product roadmap process.  As is customary in agile development, priorities (as they relate to designing and developing the EpiSync product) may shift over time. These shifting priorities will be accounted for through collaboration between the Government (Product Owner, CDC stakeholders, etc.) and the contractor to establish a shared understanding of the work, which will be planned for and communicated through standard agile practices such as product road mapping, backlog prioritization and sprint planning. For example, the CDC may decide during performance that due to changing real-life circumstances certain functionalities should be prioritized over other previously planned to be built functionalities, at which time the Government would work with the contractor to plan for and prioritize those tasks, as relevant.

Use of standard human centered design and service design practices throughout the process. This will generally include tasks such as:

Prototype and test design concepts with end users. We expect this include to formulation of testable product hypotheses, measuring of outcomes, and for the learnings to be shared with the team.

EpiSync is not a new system and is not a replacement for the National Notifiable Diseases Surveillance System (NNDSS).  Rather, EpiSync is a collection of proposed enhancements and tools to help improve NNDSS.

The scope of this work requires the contractor team to collaborate closely with a CDC EpiSync Product Owner to define and execute on specific work projects aligned with the above high-level goals.  Note that this PWS does not address all the high-level EpiSync Goals listed above. 

EpiSync Top Problems to Solve

Conducting Human Centered Design (HCD) work around the concept of Question Templates, to gather and document needs of STLTs and CDC users and other key actors and partners.  This may involve gathering and understanding existing research, as well as de-novo research with a variety of users.

The model here can be considered similar to an open-source contribution model, where outsiders can safely contribute new features to a software system.  Such cross-project fertilizations should occur under the guidance and approval of the CDC EpiSync product owner.

Product Support

The contractor shall provide product support to EpiSync users, by answering user questions, explaining features, clarifying operating instructions, providing troubleshooting tips and providing training to new and existing users, stakeholders and jurisdictions. The contractor shall solve common technical issues and work on scripts and automation to improve their workflow. Additionally, the contractor shall maintain product documentation to assist users in finding answers to questions. We expect hours of support to be between 8AM-5 PM EST. When necessary, the contractor shall escalate issues to the appropriate internal support.

The contractor shall:

Answer support questions via email messaging tools and/or chat tools

Train existing and new EpiSync users on product enablement to improve overall customer experience on an ad hoc basis to help supplement the product documentation created to assist users

Work directly with customers to provide technical support, understand customer needs, and find solutions to common problems

Work with internal teams to escalate issues, share customer feedback and make recommendations on potential product improvements

Contribute to improving consumer-facing documentation

Transition Out and Retrospective

The contractor shall work in conjunction with the Product Owner and the CDC team, as well as the incoming contractor development team (when transitioning off the project) to facilitate transition in/out activities. The contractor shall build in knowledge and document-sharing tasks into the normal sprint cadence in order to efficiently manage the transitioning on and/or off of the project. As a baseline, these activities should take place over 2 sprint cycles (4 weeks) at a minimum, although more may be needed. 

The contractor shall:

Map out transition out tasks to be incorporated over at least a 2-sprint schedule with the CDC team and the onboarding contractor

Ensure that all relevant project-specific knowledge and documentation is either shared or received before the vendor development team on this contract rolls off the project

Address any knowledge or documentation gaps that could inhibit the contractor’s ability to effectively perform the tasks/roles as outlined above in sections 1 and 2

Security Engineer: Apply full range of Information Assurance (IA) policies, principles and techniques to maintain security integrity of information systems processing sensitive or classified information. Maintain operational security posture for an information system or program. Perform threat modeling exercises. Create and maintain information system security documentation. 

Systems Architect: Experienced with, and understands the relationship between applications, operating systems, hardware and software, and developing system architectures in an enterprise setting. Experienced in creating network architecture that takes all factors of a network into consideration, such as functional requirements, technical considerations, businesses processes and end users. Ensures that all architectural products and products with architectural input are maintained in the most current state and never allowed to seriously lag or become obsolete. 

Senior Data Pipeline Engineer: Aggregates data from both internal and external data sources to build a data processing system. Creates new pipelines and builds reusable components at scale to support reporting & analytics data products. Writes complex queries to transform raw data sources into accessible models by coding. Solves complex data issues and performs root cause analysis to proactively resolve product issues. Owns the data pipeline and supports systems failures. Experienced at breaking down complex and large units of work, managing the work product of other engineers, and coaching more junior team members. Understands and has a working knowledge of HL7, FHIR, vocabulary, and HHS data standards.

Work Related to Building Blocks and CDC’s Data Modernization Initiative

Re-use of existing systems and tools, known as a building block approach, is central in CDC’s data modernization initiatives (DMI).  Therefore:

The contractor should look for opportunities to re-use existing CDC components and tools built for other projects, even if it means modifying and enhancing those components and tools to meet new needs.  This work may be done in another project’s repository, but by your contractor team.

Likewise, while guarding the integrity of the EpiSync architecture, testing, and processes, the contractor must allow for the possibility that other teams (operating under different contracts) may desire to re-use, extend, refactor, or improve upon your components and tools, to meet new needs. SECTION 6 – ADMINISTRATIVE REQUIREMENTS AND SCHEDULE OF DELIVERABLES

1.  Kick-Off Meeting

     a.  The Contractor shall attend a kick-off meeting between the Contractor’s project manager, CDC Contracting Officer, and CDC COR virtually to discuss expectations for performance and set initial priorities for the work within 10 days after award or option period effective date, if the option is exercised. For the option period, teleconference is sufficient for participants.

2.  Monthly Reporting

     a.  The Contractor shall provide a written monthly report, broken out by each task, containing the following information, at a minimum:

          (1) A brief description of tasks completed during the reporting period and significant events regarding the project.

          (2) Sufficient information for confirmation of effort covered by the invoices for the same period, workload planning, and evaluation of task performance.

     b.  The report is due to the CDC Contracting Officer’s Representative (COR) electronically (MS Word or equivalent) no later than the 10th calendar day of the month. Late delivery will delay invoice review and approval.

3.  Quality Control Plan

The Contractor shall ensure all work will be performed in accordance with the contract requirements, in compliance with the FAR 52.212-4. The contractor shall maintain and submit to the Government within 10 days after award, a complete Quality Control Plan (QCP) addressing the inspection system used to ensure the requirements of this contract are met. The contracting officer shall notify the contractor of acceptance or any required modifications.  The QCP shall include the following minimum requirements:

A description of the inspection system to cover all major services and deliverables.  The description shall include specifics as to the areas to be inspected on both a scheduled and unscheduled basis, frequency of inspections, and the title of inspectors.

Prohibition on a ByteDance Covered Application (Jun 2023)

(a) Definitions. As used in this clause—

Covered application means the social networking service TikTok or any successor application or service developed or provided by ByteDance Limited or an entity owned by ByteDance Limited.

Information technology, as defined in 40 U.S.C. 11101(6)—

(1) Means any equipment or interconnected system or subsystem of equipment, used in the automatic acquisition, storage, analysis, evaluation, manipulation, management, movement, control, display, switching, interchange, transmission, or reception of data or information by the executive agency, if the equipment is used by the executive agency directly or is used by a contractor under a contract with the executive agency that requires the use—

(i) Of that equipment; or

(ii) Of that equipment to a significant extent in the performance of a service or the furnishing of a product;

(2) Includes computers, ancillary equipment (including imaging peripherals, input, output, and storage devices necessary for security and surveillance), peripheral equipment designed to be controlled by the central processing unit of a computer, software, firmware and similar procedures, services (including support services), and related resources; but

(3) Does not include any equipment acquired by a Federal contractor incidental to a Federal contract.

(b) Prohibition. Section 102 of Division R of the Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2023 (Pub. L. 117-328), the No TikTok on Government Devices Act, and its implementing guidance under Office of Management and Budget (OMB) Memorandum M-23-13, dated February 27, 2023, “No TikTok on Government Devices” Implementation Guidance, collectively prohibit the presence or use of a covered application on executive agency information technology, including certain equipment used by Federal contractors. The Contractor is prohibited from having or using a covered application on any information technology owned or managed by the Government, or on any information technology used or provided by the Contractor under this contract, including equipment provided by the Contractor’s employees; however, this prohibition does not apply if the Contracting Officer provides written notification to the Contractor that an exception has been granted in accordance with OMB Memorandum M-23-13.

(c) Subcontracts. The Contractor shall insert the substance of this clause, including this paragraph (c), in all subcontracts, including subcontracts for the acquisition of commercial products or commercial services.

(End of clause)

As prescribed in 538.7204(b), insert the following clause:

Special Ordering Procedures for the Acquisition of Order-Level Materials (APR 2022)

(a)  Definition.

Order-level materials, as used in this clause supplies and/or services acquired in direct support of an individual task or delivery order placed against a Federal Supply Schedule (FSS) contract or FSS blanket purchase agreement (BPA), when the supplies and/or services are not known at the time of Schedule contract or FSS BPA award. The prices of order-level materials are not established in the FSS contract or FSS BPA. Order-level materials acquired following the procedures in paragraph (d) of this clause are done so under the authority of the FSS program, pursuant to 41 U.S.C. 152(3), and are not open market items, which are discussed in FAR 8.402(f).

(b) FAR 8.403(b) provides that GSA may establish special ordering procedures for a particular FSS.

(c) The procedures in FAR subpart 8.4 apply to this contract, with the exceptions listed in this clause. If a requirement in this clause is inconsistent with FAR subpart 8.4, this clause takes precedence pursuant to FAR 8.403(b).

(d)  Procedures for including order-level materials when placing an individual task or delivery order against an FSS contract or FSS BPA.

(1)  The procedures discussed in FAR 8.402(f) do not apply when placing task and delivery orders that include order-level materials.

(2)  Order-level materials are included in the definition of the term “material” in FAR clause 52.212-4 Alternate I, and, therefore, all provisions of FAR clause 52.212-4 Alternate I that apply to “materials” also apply to order-level materials.

(3)  Order-level materials shall only be acquired in direct support of an individual task or delivery order and not as the primary basis or purpose of the order.

(4) The value of order-level materials in a task or delivery order, or the cumulative value of order-level materials in orders against an FSS BPA awarded under a FSS Contract shall not exceed 33.33%.

(5)  All order-level materials shall be placed under the Order-Level Materials SIN.

(6) Prior to the placement of an order that includes order-level materials, the Ordering Activity shall follow the procedures in FAR 8.404(h).

(5)  All order-level materials shall be placed under the Order-Level Materials SIN.

(6) Prior to the placement of an order that includes order-level materials, the Ordering Activity shall follow the procedures in FAR 8.404(h).

(7)  To support the price reasonableness of order-level materials–

(i)  The Contractor proposing order-level materials as part of a solution shall obtain a minimum of three quotes for each order-level material above the simplified acquisition threshold.

(A) One of these three quotes may include materials furnished by the Contractor under paragraph (i)(1)(ii)(A) of FAR clause 52.212-4 Alternate I.

(B)  If the Contractor cannot obtain three quotes, the Contractor shall maintain its documentation of why three quotes could not be obtained to support their determination.

(C)  A Contractor with an approved purchasing system, per FAR subpart 44.3, shall instead follow its purchasing system requirement and is exempt from the requirements in paragraphs (d)(7)(i)(A) through (B) of this clause.

(ii)  The Ordering Activity Contracting Officer must make a determination that prices for all order-level materials are fair and reasonable. The Ordering Activity Contracting Officer may base this determination on a comparison of the quotes received in response to the task or delivery order solicitation or other relevant pricing information available.

(iii) If indirect costs are approved per paragraph (i)(1)(ii)(D)(2) of FAR clause 52.212-4 Alternate I, the Ordering Activity Contracting Officer must make a determination that all indirect costs approved for payment are fair and reasonable. Supporting data shall be submitted in a form acceptable to the Ordering Activity Contracting Officer.

(8) Prior to an increase in the ceiling price of order-level materials, the Ordering Activity Contracting Officer shall follow the procedures at FAR 8.404(h)(3)(iv).

(9) In accordance with GSAR clause 552.238-83, Examination of Records by GSA (Federal Supply Schedules), GSA has the authority to examine the Contractor's records for compliance with the pricing provisions in FAR clause 52.212-4 Alternate I, to include examination of any books, documents, papers, and records involving transactions related to the contract for overbillings, billing errors, and compliance with the Industrial Funding Fee (IFF) and the Sales Reporting clauses of the contract.

(10)  Order-level materials are exempt from the following clauses:

(i) 552.216-70 Economic Price Adjustment - FSS Multiple Award Schedule Contracts.

(ii) 552.238-77 Submission and Distribution of Authorized Federal Supply Schedule (FSS) Price Lists.

(iii) 552.238-81 Price Reductions.

1.0 Introduction
1.1 Purpose
The purpose of this BPA is to provide a timely and cost effective method for procuring expert
information technology services to support the CMS quality-related initiatives using agile
development iterations and methodology.
1.2 Background
CMS continues to leverage internal resources and external partnerships to fulfill the CMS
mission – as an effective steward of public funds, CMS is committed to strengthening and
modernizing the nation’s health care systems to provide access to high quality care and
improved health at lower cost.
In an effort to fulfill this charge, the CMS vision of future success is leveraging high quality
health care systems that promotes better care, access to coverage and improved health. The focus
is on measurably improving care and population health by transforming the U.S. health care
system into an integrated and accountable delivery system that continuously improves care,
reduces unnecessary costs, prevents illness and disease progression, and promotes health. CMS
forges improved and innovative mechanisms to ensure that the right care is accessible and
delivered to the right person at the right time, every time.
This effort is consistent with several initiatives to strengthen Federal acquisition practices, such
as:
• Category Management: As described in Anne Rung, the Administrator of Federal
th
Procurement Policy’s December 4 memo, which encourages strategic sourcing and
strategies to drive performance, such as developing common standards and practices
and contracts;
• US Digital Service Playbook: Released by the White House in August 2014 as best
practice guides for developing effective digital services;
• TechFAR: Released along with the Playbook, this is a guide to increase agencies use
of common agile software development services within the IT Consulting, IT
Outsourcing, and IT Security Categories; and
• Contracting Guidance to Support Modular Development: Released by the White
House on June 14, 2012

1.3 Scope
The fundamental scope of the multiple award Agile Collaboration and Modernization Endeavors
(ACME) BPA is to provide a commercial, streamlined process to support CMS quality-related
initiatives with the need for agile delivery services, including UX Design, Business Analysis,
Development, Integration, Data Management and Securitization, Agile Process Support,
Information Technology Governance and Training, and Release Management and Development
Support beneath GSA Schedule 70. These services will be available to CMS components with a
quality-related initiative and Federal agency partners sharing a collaborative CMS quality related
initiative need. ACME BPA Contractors must be able to implement applications, platforms, IT
development services and products, utilize End User Experience (UX) Design thinking and agile
software development processes that achieve results through continuous capability enhancements,
minimal downtime, prompt response to emerging needs, demonstrated reliability, and optimized
performance with resource utilization minimized.
2.0 Statement of Objectives
The objective of the multiple award ACME BPA is to advance and fortify CMS quality-related
development through the assimilation of the agile methodology by acquiring agile delivery
services for the CMS enterprise through the award of a multiple award BPA under FAR 8.4.
These services will directly or indirectly drive the delivery of information technology
development within rapid time frames based upon consistent architectures and following
standardized development guidelines to promote reuse and shareability of code and
functionality across CMS, in alignment with the Federal Digital Government Strategy 'Shared
Platform Approach' to "build once, use many times".
Specifically, the ACME BPA meets the following goals:
1. Establishes a streamlined and common contracting vehicle that:
a. Features vendors specializing in agile delivery services (e.g. all-
encompassing term that describes vendors who specialize in user
experience design and agile software development practices), user-centered
design, agile architecture (e.g. a system or software architecture that is
versatile and easy to evolve or modify), agile software development (e.g.
group of software development methods in which requirements and
solutions evolve through collaboration between self-organizing, cross-
functional teams), Cloud, and DevSecOps expertise.
b. Allows for agile delivery services as outlined in Section 2.1, Task Areas,
to be performed in a rapid-cycle fashion;
c. Includes data analytics and reporting necessary to make informed

2.1 Task Areas

technical architecture leading to the development of business applications, including
integration with existing systems. This includes expansion or updates to existing production
applications to meet the ongoing, unique objectives and requirements of specific CMS quality

with engineering and operations to implement Continuous Integration and Continuous Delivery
and testing.

Through the INFORM process, GSA will seek to share additional information with offerors in writing and/or through an oral feedback meeting that is not required by statute or regulation.  For purposes of this solicitation, and in addition to any information required by FAR Part 8.405-2(d), GSA is providing each offeror with the opportunity to participate in the INFORM process as further discussed below.  The additional opportunities set forth in this section do NOT constitute a debriefing or required debriefing pursuant to FAR 15.506.  This is a procurement being conducted pursuant to FAR Part 8 and, accordingly, any regulatory notification of award and a brief explanation of the basis for the award decision are specified at FAR 8.405-2(d).

B.	Detailed Description of the INFORM Process

B.1.	Component 1 - Notification of Award

After award and in accordance with any post-award notification timeframes required by regulation (i.e., FAR Part 8.405-2), the contracting officer will issue written notices to the successful and unsuccessful offerors (the Notification Letter and Evaluation Statement (NLES)).  The notices will contain:

●	All information required by statute or regulation;

●	An unredacted copy of the complete technical evaluation for that particular offeror that includes a full description of the unsuccessful offeror’s strengths, weaknesses, risks, and deficiencies;

●	An overall technical evaluation summary for that particular offeror and the successful offeror that includes evaluated price; overall technical ranking, rating, or score.

B.2.	Component 2 - Request for Oral Feedback Meeting or Written Questions

Within three business days after receipt of the NLES, an offeror may, but is not required to:

●	Submit a written request to the contracting officer for an oral feedback meeting; 

●	Submit a list of written questions to the contracting officer; or,

●	Take no further action.  

If an offeror does not request an oral feedback meeting or submit a list of written questions within the three-day time period, the INFORM process is concluded.  

If the offeror submits a list of written questions in lieu of the oral feedback meeting, the contracting officer will attempt to respond within five business days of receipt of the written questions.  Unless otherwise stated, the contracting officer’s response to the written questions will conclude the INFORM process.

If the offeror requests an oral feedback meeting, the offeror should provide the following information:

●	Primary point of contact;

●	List of participants with titles (e.g., Senior Vice President);

3.1 Federal Security Mandates
Federal Policies. The Contractor (and/or any subcontractor) shall comply with applicable federal
laws that include, but are not limited to, the HHS Information Security and Privacy Policy (IS2P),
CMS Information System Security & Privacy Policy (IS2P2); Federal Information Security
Modernization Act (FISMA) of 2014, (44 U.S.C. 101); National Institute of Standards and
Technology (NIST) Special Publication (SP) 800-53, Security and Privacy Controls for Federal
Information Systems and Organizations; Office of Management and Budget (OMB) Circular A-
130, Managing Information as a Strategic Resource; and other applicable federal laws,
regulations, NIST guidance, and Departmental policies.
As mandated by the Federal Information Security Modernization Act of 2014 (Public Law 113-
283) (FISMA), all federal agencies, as well as the contractors and subcontractors (hereafter
referred to as “the Contractor”) supporting those agencies, must develop, document, and
implement an Information Security (IS) program to safeguard information and information
systems. FISMA applies to any organization which has physical or electronic access to a federal
agency’s computer systems, networks, or information technology (IT) infrastructure; or uses
information systems to generate, store, process, or exchange data with, or on behalf of, a federal
agency. This includes the external third-party/outsourced and cloud hosting of agency information
or information systems.
These policies along with other federal mandates require that both contractor systems, including
Government-Owned/Contractor-Operated (GOCO) or Contractor-Owned/Contractor-Operated
(COCO) be at least as secure as government systems operated by the government. Referenced in
1. FAR Part 52 including clauses 52.239-1 and 52.204-21 (Section 4.A.) 2. FAR Subpart 39.101(c)
(Section 4.5)b.)
3.2 CMS Security & Privacy Requirements
This contract requires IT Management and System Development.
General CMS Security & Privacy Policies: All CCSQ contractors, regardless of the contract size,
program objectives, funding, or other factors, are subjected to applicable Department of Health
and Human Services (HHS) and CMS Security & Privacy policies.

3.2.1 General CMS Security & Privacy Policies
This section provides an overview of general CMS Security policies and outlines requirements
applicable to all CMS contractors and subcontractors. The CMS Information Security and Privacy
Program website provides additional details of CMS security policies and procedures across CMS,
and is referenced throughout this document.
The Contractor must adhere to all CMS and federal IT Security and Privacy standards, policies,
statutes, and reporting requirements, as well as all National Institute of Standards and Technology
(NIST) standards and guidelines, and other Government-wide laws and regulations for the
protection and security of Government Information.
The Contractor must also adhere to the guidance and requirements provided within the CMS
Information Systems Security and Privacy Policy (IS2P2). The IS2P2 consolidates existing
information security and privacy policy documents into a single volume and directly integrates the
enforcement of information security and privacy through the CMS CIO, Chief Information
Security Officer, and Senior Official for Privacy.
The IS2P2 and other relevant documents can be obtained from the CMS Information Security and
Privacy Library. The Contractor must review the CMS Information Security and Privacy Program
website and Information Security and Privacy Library at minimum every 30-calendar days for
updates.
The Contractor must ensure all applicable federal privacy requirements are being met, including,
but not limited to, Privacy Act System of Records Notification (SORN), Privacy Impact
Assessments (PIAs), Data Use Agreements (DUAs), and Computer Matching Agreements
(CMAs) in accordance with CMS procedures available on the CMS Information Security and
Privacy Library.
Data & Records Management
The Contractor assumes responsibility for protection of the confidentiality of government records
and must ensure that all work performed by its employees and subcontractors is under the
supervision of the Contractor. Each Contractor employee or any of its subcontractors to whom
any CMS records may be made available or disclosed must be notified in writing by the Contractor
that information disclosed to such employee or subcontractor can be used only for that purpose
and to the extent authorized herein.

• Role-based Training
All Contractor employees with significant security responsibilities (as determined by the
Program Manager) must complete role-based training annually, commensurate with their
role and responsibilities, and in accordance with HHS and CMS policies.
• Training Records
The Contractor must maintain training records for all its employees working under this
contract in accordance with HHS and CMS policies, and supply them to CMS upon request.
Rules of Behavior
All Contractor employees must adhere to all HHS, CMS and QNet Rules of Behavior (ROB) before
accessing data or other information, systems, and/or networks that store/process government
information. Initially at the beginning of the contract, and at least annually thereafter, any
contractor working on CMS systems or with CMS data must provide a signed statement attesting
to the fact that it understands and will abide by the HHS ROB. This may be done as part of annual
OpDiv Information Security Awareness Training. If the training is provided by the Contractor, the
signed ROB must be provided as a separate deliverable to the Contracting Officer (CO) and/or
Contracting Officer’s Representative (COR).
Incident Response & Reporting
FISMA defines an incident as “an occurrence that (1) actually or imminently jeopardizes, without
lawful authority, the integrity, confidentiality, or availability of information or an information
system; or (2) constitutes a violation or imminent threat of violation of law, security policies,
security procedures, or acceptable use policies.”
A privacy breach is a type of incident and is defined by FISMA as the loss of control, compromise,
unauthorized disclosure, unauthorized acquisition, or any similar occurrence where (1) a person
other than an authorized user accesses or potentially accesses PII, or (2) an authorized user accesses
or potentially accesses PII for other than an authorized purpose.
The Contractor must ensure that all suspected information security and privacy incidents are
reported within 1 hour of identifying the suspected incident. Contractors utilizing HCQIS systems
must report all incidents in accordance with the QualityNet Incident Response Procedures (which
can be provided upon request). All other contractors must report all suspected incidents to the CMS
IT Service Helpdesk in accordance with the Risk Management Handbook (RMH) Chapter 08:
Incident Response (available on the CMS Information Security and Privacy Library).

CMS Control Implementation and Baselines: Acceptable Risk Safeguards (ARS)
The CMS Information Security and Privacy Acceptable Risk Safeguards (ARS) provides guidance
to CMS and its contractors as to the minimum acceptable level of required security controls that
must be implemented by CMS and CMS contractors to protect CMS’ information and information
systems, including CMS Sensitive Information. The ARS is based on:
• National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) Special Publication (SP) 800-53
Revision 4 (NIST SP 800-53r4), Security and Privacy Controls for Federal Information
Systems and Organizations, dated April 2013
• Federal Risk and Authorization Management Program (FedRAMP)
• HHS Information Systems Security and Privacy Policy (IS2P)
• CMS Information Systems Security and Privacy Policy (CMS IS2P2) CMS-CIO-POL-
SEC-2016-0001
• CMS policies, procedures, and guidance
• Other federal and non-federal guidance resources
• Industry-leading information security and privacy practices adopted by CMS
Security Assessment and Authorization (SA&A) Process
HCQIS contracts that require the Contractor to build or support application/systems development
to meet CMS/CCSQ program-specific goals are required to fully support CMS FISMA system’s
SA&A efforts for the application or system. Application Development Organizations (ADOs)
should attempt to utilize any of CMS’ Enterprise Services when applicable to help reduce cost and
effort required to implement proven solutions, apply security controls, and achieve agency
accreditation for a system or application. In efforts to support CMS SA&A, the Contractor must:
1. Comply with ATO requirements as mandated by federal laws and policies, including
making available any documentation, physical access, and logical access needed to support
this requirement. The level of effort for the ATO is based on the System’s NIST FIPS 199
categorization and CMS procedures (located on the CMS Information Security and Privacy
Program website).
2. Coordinate with the CMS Business Owner to create, maintain, and update all applicable
ATO documentation as defined by CMS Information Security procedures.
3. Obtain an independent Security Controls Assessment (SCA) for all CMS systems and
infrastructures in accordance with CMS procedures located on the CMS Information
Security and Privacy Program website. The SCA is a detailed evaluation of the controls
protecting an information system. It determines the extent to which controls are
implemented correctly, operating as intended, and producing the desired outcome with
respect to meeting the security requirements for the system. Requirements for control
assessments are described in the CMS Acceptable Risk Safeguards (ARS) and can be found
within the CMS Information Security and Privacy Library.
4. Allow CMS employees (or CMS CISO-designated third-party Contractors) to conduct
SCA activities, to include control reviews, in accordance with NIST SP 800-53/NIST SP
800-53A and CMS procedures and standards (located on the CMS Information Security
and Privacy Program website).

For security purposes, information may be sensitive because it requires security to protect its
confidentiality, integrity, and/or availability. The Contractor must protect all government
information that is or may be sensitive in accordance with OMB Memorandum M-06-16,
Protection of Sensitive Agency Information by securing it with a FIPS 140-2 validated solution.
The Contractor must perform the following activities to ensure that any information generated,
collected, or provided to the Contractor is properly maintained:
1. Protect government information and information systems in order to ensure:
• Confidentiality, which means preserving authorized restrictions on access and
disclosure based on the security terms found in this contract, including means for
protecting personal privacy and proprietary information;
• Integrity, which means guarding against improper information modification or
destruction, and ensuring information non-repudiation and authenticity; and
• Availability, which means ensuring timely and reliable access to, and use of,
information.
2. Comply with the HHS Standard for Encryption of Computing Devices and Information to
prevent unauthorized access to government information (available on the CMS Information
Security and Privacy Library). Encrypt all sensitive federal data and information
(including, but not limited to, Personally Identifiable Information (PII), Protected Health
Information (PHI), proprietary information, and Federal Tax Information (FTI)) in transit
(i.e., via email, network connections, etc.) and at rest (i.e., on servers, storage devices,
mobile devices, backup media, etc.) with a FIPS 140-2 validated encryption solution.
3. Secure all devices (including, but not limited to, desktops, laptops, and mobile devices)
that store and process government information, and ensure devices meet HHS and CMS-
specific encryption standard requirements. Maintain a complete and current inventory of
all laptop computers, desktop computers, and other mobile devices and portable media that
store or process sensitive government information (including PII).
4. Contractors connecting approved Contractor Furnished Equipment (CFE) to the CMS-
owned network must ensure proper security posture and controls are in place to ensure that
the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of CMS-owned data is maintained at all times.
Contractors connecting to the Health Care Quality Information Systems (HCQIS) Network
must comply with all requirements conveyed within the Health Care Quality Information
Systems Contractor-Furnished Equipment (CFE) Guidelines and Requirements document,

3.2.2 IT Management and IT Systems Development
Implementation of FISMA mandates that all HHS/CMS information systems require an Agency-
approved Authorization to Operate (ATO) based on NIST Special Publication (SP) 800-37, Guide
for Applying the Risk Management Framework to Federal Information Systems.
FISMA requirements apply to CMS contracts that involve utilization of IT resources that require
maintenance, development, or management of CMS information systems or data. Further defined,
IT Management and IT Systems Development may include, but is not limited to, operations and
maintenance of CMS IT Infrastructures, General Support Systems (GSS), and networks that
process, store, and/or transmit CMS data. Additionally, this includes the creation, development, or
support of application/systems to meet CMS program-specific goals. These contracts are subject
to CMS FISMA and System Authorization and Assessment (SA&A) requirements as defined
within the CMS Risk Management Handbook and CMS’ Acceptable Risk Safeguards (ARS) (which
are both available on the CMS Information Security and Privacy Library).
The Contractor responsible for the system(s)/application(s) must obtain and/or maintain a valid
ATO as determined by the CMS Program Business Owner and ISSO. The ATO must be authorized
by the CMS CIO before a system goes into operation or begins processing CMS information. The
failure to obtain and maintain a valid ATO may be grounds for termination of a contract.
