(a) Definitions. As used in this provision—
Backhaul, covered telecommunications equipment or services, critical technology,
interconnection arrangements, reasonable inquiry, roaming, and substantial or essential
component have the meanings provided in the clause 52.204-25, Prohibition on Contracting for
Certain Telecommunications and Video Surveillance Services or Equipment.
(b) Prohibition.
(1) Section 889(a)(1)(A) of the John S. McCain National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal
Year 2019 (Pub. L. 115-232) prohibits the head of an executive agency on or after August 13,
2019, from procuring or obtaining, or extending or renewing a contract to procure or obtain, any
equipment, system, or service that uses covered telecommunications equipment or services as a
substantial or essential component of any system, or as critical technology as part of any system.
Nothing in the prohibition shall be construed to—
(i) Prohibit the head of an executive agency from procuring with an entity to provide a
service that connects to the facilities of a third-party, such as backhaul, roaming, or
interconnection arrangements; or
(ii) Cover telecommunications equipment that cannot route or redirect user data traffic
or cannot permit visibility into any user data or packets that such equipment transmits or
otherwise handles.
(2) Section 889(a)(1)(B) of the John S. McCain National Defense Authorization Act for
Fiscal Year 2019 (Pub. L. 115-232) prohibits the head of an executive agency on or after August
13, 2020, from entering into a contract or extending or renewing a contract with an entity that
uses any equipment, system, or service that uses covered telecommunications equipment or
services as a substantial or essential component of any system, or as critical technology as part of
any system. This prohibition applies to the use of covered telecommunications equipment or
services, regardless of whether that use is in performance of work under a Federal contract.
Nothing in the prohibition shall be construed to—
(i) Prohibit the head of an executive agency from procuring with an entity to provide a
service that connects to the facilities of a third-party, such as backhaul, roaming, or
interconnection arrangements; or
(ii) Cover telecommunications equipment that cannot route or redirect user data traffic
or cannot permit visibility into any user data or packets that such equipment transmits or
otherwise handles.
(c) Procedures. The Offeror shall review the list of excluded parties in the System for Award
Management (SAM) (https://www.sam.gov) for entities excluded from receiving federal awards
for "covered telecommunications equipment or services".

Request for Capability Statement
1. Title: Modern Claim Adjudication Development Services 2.0 (MCADS 2.0)
2. Description: This is a Request for Capability Statement. This is NOT a
solicitation for proposals, proposal abstracts, or quotations. The purpose
of this request is to obtain knowledge and information for project planning
purposes.
Purpose: The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) seeks to grow and
scale the Medicare Adjudication Portal (MAP), a transformative new system designed to
revolutionize the processing of more than 1 billion original Medicare (Parts A and B)
claims annually. This procurement aims to obtain services for the development,
implementation, and operation of MAP as the future foundation of Medicare claims
processing, featuring real-time adjudication capabilities that align with the Medicare
Payment System Modernization (MPSM) vision and roadmap. The new system
developed under this contract shall serve as the cornerstone of a modernized Medicare
claims processing ecosystem, enabling real-time processing and providing a scalable
platform for systematic expansion to additional claim types.
For the purposes of this effort, the Medicare Payment System encompasses all products
and systems that work together to process and adjudicate claims for the Original (Fee-
for-Service) Medicare program, including facilitating alternative payment models and
aspects of value-based care. The Medicare Adjudication Portal (MAP) represents the
next generation of this integrated ecosystem, designed as a cloud-native platform
capable of real-time claims processing and adjudication. CMS is modernizing the entire
Medicare Payment System through its MPSM initiative, with MAP serving as the
foundational technology that will replace legacy systems while maintaining operational
continuity during the transition.
The Modern Claims Adjudication Development Services 2.0 (MCADS 2.0) contract
provides comprehensive development and implementation support for building MAP.
This contract specifically procures cross-functional scrum teams that will develop,
deploy, and operate the platform, initially handling claims submitted by the Medicare

Purpose and Requirements
Purpose
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) seeks to grow and scale the Medicare
Adjudication Portal (MAP), a transformative new system designed to revolutionize the processing of
more than 1 billion original Medicare (Parts A and B) claims annually. This procurement aims to
obtain services for the development, implementation, and operation of MAP as the future
foundation of Medicare claims processing, featuring real-time adjudication capabilities that align
with the Medicare Payment System Modernization (MPSM) vision and roadmap. The new system
developed under this contract shall serve as the cornerstone of a modernized Medicare claims
processing ecosystem, enabling real-time processing and providing a scalable platform for
systematic expansion to additional claim types.
For the purposes of this effort, the Medicare Payment System encompasses all products and systems
that work together to process and adjudicate claims for the Original (Fee-for-Service) Medicare
program, including facilitating alternative payment models and aspects of value-based care. The
Medicare Adjudication Portal (MAP) represents the next generation of this integrated ecosystem,
designed as a cloud-native platform capable of real-time claims processing and adjudication. CMS is
modernizing the entire Medicare Payment System through its MPSM initiative, with MAP serving as
the foundational technology that will replace legacy systems while maintaining operational
continuity during the transition.
The Modern Claims Adjudication Development Services 2.0 (MCADS 2.0) contract provides
comprehensive development and implementation support for building MAP. This contract
speci�ically procures cross-functional scrum teams that will develop, deploy, and operate the
platform, initially handling claims submitted by the Medicare Administrative Contractors (MACs)

software engineering practices and modern Cloud computing infrastructure. This team should

Architecture, Data Engineering, and Systems Integration specialists.

Purpose of the RFI
Solicit feedback from potential vendors with experience developing and deploying similar
solutions.
1. Obtain a variety of options for our modern data platform moving forward and build an
understanding of platforms to implement implementations.
2. Understand the relative number of vendors in the market capable of providing a solution
comparable to the solution’s functionality defined in this RFI.
3. Obtain information to develop a Request for Proposal (RFP) to solicit a vendor capable of
providing the solution defined in the RFI.
Current State
The City of Minneapolis currently leverages an on-premises platform combining multiple on-
premises SQL servers, databases, and data marts. Data from City source systems is integrated
into our on-premises data marts through ETL and made available to end users through semantic
modeling. Data users at the City utilize self-service reporting through this to build reports and
visualizations to support informed decision making and data transparency efforts.
The current data reporting platform consists of the following components:
• Data marts: Multiple data marts are set up to serve as repositories for various content
areas across City of Minneapolis businesses. The topic areas include public safety, non-
CJIS public safety, open data, property and addressing, and general City business. These
data marts are set up through on-premises Microsoft SQL Server.
• ETL and Enterprise Service Bus: DataStage is utilized as the City’s primary Extract
Transform Load (ETL) tool. This is primarily used for overnight ETL from business systems
into data marts. MuleSoft is the City’s primary Enterprise Service Bus for integrations
across systems and is being built out to connect to reporting APIs from cloud and SAAS
business systems to bring required information into our reporting environments.
• Reporting and visualization tools: Semantic models are set up through Cognos Framework
Manager to allow self-service drag and drop reporting through Cognos Analytics. Data
visualization and reporting is primarily done through Tableau Desktop and Tableau Server
connected to reports generated from Cognos Analytics. ArcGIS Enterprise is a primary
mapping tool used by the City, and Power BI can be used for data visualization.
High-Level Functional Elements
The desired future state has specific functional elements. Respondents should provide detailed
information regarding their solution and capabilities to meet these functional elements.

Performance Work Statement

Quality Data and Analytics Services (QDAS II)

Data Analytics Supporting Healthcare (DASH)

Blanket Purchase Agreement (BPA)

Contents

1.0 Introduction

1.1 Purpose

The goal of the Enterprise QualityNet Data and Analytic Services Centralized Data Repository (CDR) and CCSQ Analytics Platform is to provide next generation data storage and analytical CCSQ Analytics Platform abilities to the CCSQ data scientists and contractor community to support their data research and analyses related to the Center’s goals for measuring clinical quality and leading improvement initiatives.

The Quality Data and Analytics Services (QDAS)II Performance Work Statement (PWS) Statement addresses operations and maintenance (O&M), as well as continuous technology and business process improvements of the CCSQ’s Enterprise Data and Analytic services, including IT management and governance, business process analysis, Human-Centered Design (HCD), user research, community engagement, training and coaching, agile solution architecture and design, agile application development, configuration and innovation, analytics and reporting, integration with other CMS and CCSQ applications, using modern software development methodologies that combine software development and operations (DevOps), as well as Machine Learning (MLOps).

Through this SOO, CCSQ seeks to expand data offerings in CDR and enhance analytic tools based on the business requirements and user community needs. Continuous innovation and improvements of the core technologies in the operational environment, as well as business processes related to data and analytic activities in CCSQ, is important part of this work.

1.2 Assumptions and Constraints

The Contractor will provide qualified staff with necessary technical competencies to support specialized data and analytic projects in CCSQ, including but not limited to:

Project management according to Agile best practices, system design, deployments, help desk support, application testing, system outage monitoring, troubleshooting and triaging defects and environment issues, user research and persona development based on HCD best practices, user acceptance testing (UAT), and supporting system maintenance and patching tasks.

Description of CDR Services

Data Architecture Management

Define and maintain CCSQ Data Technology Architecture, utilizing Machine Learning and Artificial Intelligence to build the data objects, tables, views, and models. Learn, adjust, alert, and recommend improvements, making the data & analytics community who administer and use the environment more efficient and effective.

Define and maintain CCSQ Data Integration Architecture. To the maximum feasible extent, enable adaptability and flexibility, to allow managing data flows by creating a series of interconnected and bidirectional data pipelines that serve various business needs. The pipelines are constructed using base data objects—data snapshots, data increments, data views, reference data, master data, and flat, subject-oriented tables. The data objects serve as building blocks that can be reused / repurposed, to ensure the steady flow of high quality, relevant data to the business.

Develop and maintain relevant data architecture artifacts such as Data Roadmaps, Technology Roadmaps, data flow diagrams, logical and physical cloud configuration, etc.

Promote alignment with Enterprise data strategy, ISG OKRs and CDR architectural goals.

Support data engineering guidance, technical direction and best practices for CDR services.

Administer AWS Data Migration and Transfer Services (such as Glue, DMS).

Administer AWS Database Services (such as RDS, RedShift).

Track metered cloud resources usage to optimize performance and reduce costs.

Establish and adhere to Service Level Agreements (SLA’s) related to cloud resources’ Key Performance Indicators (KPI’s), such as Recovery Point Objective (RPO), Recovery Time Objective (RTO), Mean Time to Repair (MTTR).

ATTACHMENT A
Background
The City of Minneapolis is preparing to embark on a data modernization project that will entail
moving our data, reporting, and analytics infrastructure from our current, on-premises
architecture and tools to modern cloud data implementation. The data modernization project is a
requirement of the Settlement that the City of Minneapolis entered into with the Minnesota
Department of Human Rights focused on biased policing practices in the MPD. The settlement
requires that the City contract with an objective, third party to conduct a data and technology
assessment. That assessment was conducted by Accenture and their recommendation, among
other recommendations, was to move to a cloud analytic environment, specifically a Microsoft
environment. The City is open to other technologies as recommended in any response to this RFI.
The City is looking to extend these capabilities beyond the public safety realm to encompass an
enterprise-wide reporting strategy. Respondents should consider the City’s need to work with
both public safety CJIS data and non-public safety data.
Vision
Usable, trustworthy data and a robust analytic platform and toolset is foundational to the City of
Minneapolis in becoming a more data driven, transparent City. Our data and analytic environment
needs to be usable to a broad group of users, allowing them to leverage modern tools to facilitate
the creation of insights. The environment should also be designed to be resilient for future needs
and architectures.
Principles
• Shift existing data infrastructure and tooling to a modern cloud platform that will:
o Increase compliance and CJIS security measures.
o Improve data reliability, availability, and scalability.
• Implement strong data acquisition, governance, and quality practices to prepare and
leverage reliable data for reporting and advanced analytics use cases.
• Grow data & analytics capabilities beyond compliance requirements.
• Make scalable decisions that can benefit other departments long-term.
• Continue to empower City departments with their data, analytics, and reporting.

REQUEST FOR INFORMATION
FOR
DATA MODERNIZATION
I. INVITATION: The City of Minneapolis (“City”) invites interested parties to respond to this Request
for Information (“RFI”) regarding information and recommendations related to the City’s
migration of its current on-premises data tooling and infrastructure to a data cloud environment.
This would include recommendations such as implementation, migration, and on-going support.
The City has a desired future state with specific functional elements. Respondents should provide
detailed information regarding their solution and capabilities to meet these functional elements.
By moving to a more modern data infrastructure and tooling, we expect to unlock a range of
strategic benefits and technical improvements.
This RFI is strictly an inquiry. No contract or agreement will be entered into as a result of this
process. However, the information contained in the RFI responses will help the City advance its
data modernization effort which will result in the release an Request For Proposal (RFP) in the
future.
II. BACKGROUND INFORMATION:
The City of Minneapolis is preparing to embark on a data modernization project that will entail
moving our data, reporting, and analytics infrastructure from our current, on-premises
architecture and tools to modern cloud data implementation. The data modernization project is
a requirement of the Settlement that the City of Minneapolis entered into with the Minnesota
Department of Human Rights focused on biased policing practices in the MPD. The settlement
requires that the City contract with an objective, third party to conduct a data and technology
assessment. That assessment was conducted by Accenture and their recommendation, among
other recommendations, was to move to a cloud analytic environment, specifically a Microsoft
environment. The City is open to other technologies as recommended in any response to this RFI.
The City is looking to extend these capabilities beyond the public safety realm to encompass an
enterprise-wide reporting strategy. Respondents should consider the City’s need to work with
both public safety CJIS data and non-public safety data.
III. SCOPE OF SERVICES or RFI OBJECTIVE
Solicit feedback from potential vendors with experience developing and deploying similar
solutions.
1. Obtain a variety of options for our modern data platform moving forward and build an
understanding of platforms to implement implementations.
2. Understand the relative number of vendors in the market capable of providing a
solution comparable to the solution’s functionality defined in this RFI.

Performance Work Statement

Rural Development Data Engineering Branch Service and Support

Background

As a critical component of United States Department of Agriculture (USDA), the Rural Development (RD) Mission Area is driven by a mission to stimulate, grow, and protect the American rural economy by providing loans, grants, and loan guarantees to individuals and commercial entities engaged in the betterment of rural life. As such, RD generates massive amounts of structured and unstructured data. The USDA Rural Development Technology Office (RDTO) requires qualified contractors or teams to assist in analyzing, solutioning, developing and implementing modern data management methods and technologies to support the RD mission.

Scope of Work

Insert the scope of the work required. Describe the general scope of the work to be performed in relatively brief terms.  Consider a balance between a broad scope and narrowly defined scope. Future changes to the task order will have to be “within scope”. 

RD requires qualified contractors to provide maintenance and modernization support and services for the RD Data Management efforts. These support and services will provide enhancements to the Data Warehouse, Geospatial environments, and Enterprise Reporting environments

Goal / Outcome / Objective

The objective of this requirement is to gain the support and services of qualified contractors or teams to assist in analyzing, solutioning, developing and implementing modern data management methods and technologies to support the following focus areas. 

Focus Area 1: Data Warehouse Support and Services

Focus Area 2: Enterprise Reporting Support and Services

Focus Area 3: Geospatial Support and Services

Technical Requirements

 Focus Area 1: Data Warehouse Support and Services

Focus Area 1: Data Warehouse Support and Services

Focus Area 2: Enterprise Reporting Support and Services

Focus Area 3: Geospatial Support and Services

Technical Requirements

 Focus Area 1: Data Warehouse Support and Services

RD’s data landscape includes Structured Data, Semi-Structured Data, Unstructured Data and Geospatial Data.  All of these data types must be considered in any efforts taken under this Focus Area. RD has an established Oracle data warehouse with data stored in a tabular format. It is used for report generation and data analysis and is considered a core component of RD’s current business intelligence capability. The Tabular Data Warehouse stores data from disparate sources, and maintenance includes tasks such as resolving load issues (both push/pull and internal/external), report changes, data corrections and updates, data formatting and migrations, ETL maintenance and testing, risk identification and mitigation, data dictionary maintenance, and task tracking. Modernization includes tasks such as designs consistent with a federal enterprise architecture framework providing conceptual and logical data models for industry-focused business segments such as loan origination, loan servicing, or loan accounting; analysis of a current state risks and mitigation recommendations; analysis and solution architecture recommendations for RD’s future state data management environment (expected to use Databricks), based on RD’s future state data architecture vision; developing and implementing strategies for implementation of RD’s future state, associated governance, and standard non-functional requirements for inclusion in various solicitations.

Data needs to be structured and stored for optimal utilization by our existing and future Business Intelligence (BI) tools. Underlying data structures in the TDW need to change to increase BI tool’s abilities to pull the data elements that program areas need. There are data quality issues pulled from the source systems. Data corrections need to be made to ensure reported data are accurate. The focus should be on the access and curation of data. Required support may include, but is not limited to:

Collect requirements and integrate data warehouse changes to support existing schema and the creation of new schema for program area data analytics and business intelligence reporting.

Assess the current system, tools, and processes in place, or potentially already available to RD (e.g. EDAPT, USDA shared service, or resources.data.gov), for populating, maintaining, facilitating data access, utilization, and reporting from the TDW and implement recommend improvements or upgrades to the data marts or the TDW.

Establish and maintain Data Warehouse operations performance metrics and indicators to support ongoing operations.

Apply ML/NLP techniques for generating custom analytic data finder files for CCSQ user community that help identify patient or study populations.

Tableau

Examples of required Contractor tasks that may be required include: 

Provide business intelligence maintenance that supports day-to-day operations and develop applications that provide data utilization from the Data Warehouse.

Make updates to reports and dashboards.

Collect requirements for business intelligence reporting updates to existing reports and the creation of new reports.

Plan for and oversee the deployment of data to the Data Warehouse from previously non-integrated source systems.

Develop business intelligence dashboards and associated technical guidance.

Develop policies and procedures around the collection, use, and preservation of data.

Provide coordination and support for data stewards including writing procedures and/or training materials on data management, governance, and curation.

Support Data Science and/or Business Intelligence future needs

 Focus Area 3: Geospatial Environment Support and Services

Geospatial integration and capacity building are integral to the business operations and mission delivery of RD. RD has plans to expand integrated geospatial capabilities into enterprise online loan application systems, as well as loan underwriting systems. The geospatial analysis and data collected by these systems enables RD to streamline the processes of determining loan eligibility, the loan approval process, and improved reporting and outreach capabilities.  The full analysis of program delivery and impact requires geospatial data and analysis (e.g., analysis of the expansion of broadband access through RD funded project service areas). Geospatial analysis enhances statistical analysis giving RD the ability to fully understand the relationships, patterns, and trends in the data. RD has implemented the full ArcGIS for Enterprise technology stack.

Contractor maintenance and modernization support and services of the geospatial infrastructure and data are necessary to support enterprise system integrations, data warehousing, and reporting requirements. Required Contractor tasks may include but are not limited to the maintenance of current geospatial infrastructure and capabilities, and deployment of new technologies as requested.

Program Management

Program and Project Management

The contractor shall provide program management support under this TO. This includes the management and oversight of all activities performed by contractor personnel, including subcontractors, to satisfy the requirements identified in this PWS.

Program Management Plan (PMP)

Develop and deliver a Draft and Final Program/Project Management Plan (PMP). The PMP is a living document which the Contractor shall update throughout the Period of Performance (POP) with weekly activity information and submit to the Government for approval.

Project Schedule

Develop and maintain an integrated Program/Project Schedule that includes activities and tasks, dependencies, resources and interrelationships.

Monthly Status Reports

Provide Monthly Consolidated/Status report of monthly activities and tasks, revisions and updates to project plan. Monthly status reports must be in writing and submitted by the due date via email.

The Monthly Consolidated/Status Report details that month’s activities, tasks and accomplishments, and shall include:

Overall status

Spending burn rate

Major accomplishments that support USDA for the reporting period as compared to the work plan tasks and milestones.

Outline of the major goals for the coming reporting period to include due dates

Next months planned activities and tasks

Other Reports as Requested

As the project progresses there may be other reports or presentations that may be required.

Coordinate and Plan Meetings

Coordinate and plan meetings, including documenting meeting discussions and action items, developing relevant white papers, and creating presentations or demonstrations, as needed.

 Contract Management

The Contractor shall establish clear organizational lines of authority and responsibility to ensure effective management of the resources assigned to performance of the contract. 

The Contractor shall establish processes and assign appropriate resources to effectively administer the contract. The Contractor shall respond to Government requests for contractual actions in a timely fashion. The Contractor shall have a single point of contact between the Government and Contractor personnel assigned to support task orders. The Contractor shall assign work effort and maintaining proper and accurate time keeping records of personnel assigned to work on the requirement.

The Contractor shall be responsible for any subcontract management necessary to integrate work performed on this requirement and shall be responsible and accountable for subcontractor performance on this requirement. The prime Contractor will manage work distribution to ensure there are no Organizational Conflict of Interest (OCI) considerations.

Performance Measures

 Quality Assurance Surveillance Plan (QASP)

The QASP is used in performance-based acquisitions and will be used for each Task order. The QASP describes measurable performance standards and methods used by the government to provide surveillance and oversight of the contractor’s quality controls efforts to ensure timely, effective, and delivery results as specified in the PWS. 

The contractor shall establish and maintain a complete Quality Control Plan (QCP) as identified in the BPA and subsequent Task orders as a required deliverable. Quality Management shall be utilized to ensure the services are performed in accordance with the contract and commonly accepted commercial practices. The contractor shall develop and implement procedures to identify, prevent and ensure non-recurrence of defective services. The government reserves the right to perform inspections on services provided to the extent deemed necessary to protect the government’s interests. The contractor must control the quality of the services and deliverables provided in support of this contract and subsequent Task orders and maintain substantiating evidence that services conform to contract and Task order quality requirements and furnish such information to the government if requested. The QCP shall include a Performance Requirements Summary (PRS) such as the sample PRS shown in the following table. The PRS shall reflect the quality approach (using the headings shown in the table below) of the contractor as it applies to the key areas of the Task order.

The following PRS is a sample format for the contractor only. The Contractor shall propose a PRS (using the headings shown in the sample) as part of the QCP in response to each Task order to be finalized 10 days after the Task order kick-off meeting reflecting the key areas of support identified in the Task order and the QCP proposed by the contractor. 

The mutually agreed upon PRS shall be incorporated into the QCP template to be finalized within 10 days of each Task order kick-off.

Deliverables

Most deliverables require at least two iterations – a Draft and a Final. All written deliverables must be submitted as Draft version prior to Final version. The Government will review and provide written feedback on the deliverable to the Contractor. The Contractor shall address all Government comments in the Final version of the document. The Government will have ten (10) business days to review and comment on each deliverable. The Contractor will have five (5) days to incorporate the Government comments. 

The Government reserves the right to reject deliverables outright without review for those deliverables that are not “well formed”. Deliverable formatting, structure and required content for each deliverable will be determined by the Government and the Contractor during the Project Kick‐Off period. All deliverables must meet Section 508 standards for accessibility.

General quality measures, as set forth below, will be applied to each work product received from the Contractor.

Accuracy – Work products shall be accurate in presentation, technical content, and adherence to accepted elements of style.

Clarity – Work products shall be clear and concise; all diagrams shall be easy to understand and be relevant to the supporting narrative.

Consistency to Requirements – Work products shall satisfy the requirements of the TO.

File Editing ‐ All text and diagrammatic files shall be editable by the Government.

Creativity – Devise actionable workarounds and solutions to problems encountered.

The contractor shall deliver all electronic versions of deliverables by email or removable electronic media (as specified by the Government), as well as placing deliverables in USDA’s designated repository (often SharePoint). The following are the required electronic formats, whose versions must be compatible with the latest, commonly available version on the market.

Text - Commonly used word processing software compatible with Microsoft Word or searchable PDF

Spreadsheets - CSV or tab delimited format compatible with Microsoft Excel

Briefings - Compatible with Microsoft PowerPoint

Drawings - Compatible with Microsoft Visio and/or searchable PDF

Schedules - Compatible with Microsoft Project and/or searchable PDF

Dashboards - Linkage to USDA technologies Tableau Dashboard Files

Evaluate current-state architecture, identify technical and operational gaps, data duplication, and optimal efficiencies for CMS. 

Develop a comprehensive integration strategy that aligns with federal data mandates, ISG business objectives, and CMS Cloud infrastructure.  

Assess opportunities for integration with CMS Cloud services to support long-term scalability, security, and operational efficiency. 

Contribute to a solution plan that includes clearly defined milestones and timelines, implementation timelines and impacts to current analytics space.

Task Area 4 (Non-Core Services/Optional Task) – User Support Services

CCSQ currently offers a wide variety of coaching and support options available to business and technical users, including CMS central and regional staff, other CMS/HHS components and contractors. See Appendix F DASH BPA PWS, section 2.4 Task Area 4 Training, for more information.

The Contractor shall provide user support services, including but not limited to the following:

Provide technical guidance, training and aid to the CCSQ teams seeking to use CDR and CCSQ Analytics Platform  services.

Act as a primary Customer 360 Support hub related to data and analytics in CCSQ. Collaborate closely with other teams in ISG and CCSQ/CMS who provide support to CCSQ data and analytics user community.

Provide temporary assistance to the teams seeking to use CDR/CCSQ Analytics Platform services ensure organizations are successful using data and analytic services independently (as self- service).

Promote team and organization level support in addition to 1-on-1 user support. Provide team consultation services about best suitable tools and solutions in CDR/ CCSQ Analytics Platform that can help with program data needs and analyses.

Provide strategic guidance and support to the organizational units within CCSQ, including different groups within the Center (i.e. QIIG, QMVIG, etc.) and different contractor teams and user communities.

Optimize the DAR intake process to streamline requesting CDR/ CCSQ Analytics Platform consultation support allowing CMS to easily prioritize and respond to requests.

Establish early user outreach procedures to gain understanding about community’s needs rather than solely replying on requests and issues submitted by users.

Drive customer-centric initiatives and experiences to develop a deeper understanding of users’ wants, needs and motivations.

Evaluate user needs related to the current and future analytic tools/data offerings in CDR/ CCSQ Analytics Platform to deliver personalized experiences.

Data Rights

The Government asserts Unlimited Rights to all data produced by the contractor in performance of this PWS. Unlimited Rights as used in this clause, means the rights of the Government to use, disclose, reproduce, prepare derivative works, distribute copies to the public, including by electronic means, and perform publicly and display publicly, in any manner, including by electronic means, and for any purpose whatsoever, and to have or permit others to do so. The contractor agrees not to assert copyright in computer software produced in the performance of this contract without prior written permission of the Government. When such permission is granted, the Government shall specify appropriate terms, conditions, and submission requirements to assure utilization, dissemination, and commercialization of the data. The contractor, when requested, shall promptly deliver to the Government a duly executed and approved instrument fully confirmatory of all rights to which the Government is entitled.

None of this data will be released to any other Government organization or other organizations of individuals without the express written approval of USDA unless otherwise specified.

REQUEST FOR PROPOSALS
RFP 26-02-CD
AI-POWERED DEVELOPMENT SERVICES OPTIMIZATION PLATFORM
Advertisement and Proposal Instructions
The City of Destin will receive proposals for an AI-Powered Development Services Optimization Platform.
The project is located in the City of Destin at 4200 Indian Bayou Trail.
As outlined in this RFP, the selected vendor shall provide an integrated AI-assisted platform supporting
Development Services functions including plan review efficiency, Land Development Code (LDC)
digitization, staff knowledge systems, and workflow optimization.
To obtain a copy of this RFP, vendors must contact the City Clerk’s Office at cityclerk@cityofdestin.com.
Please include “Request for Proposal Documents – RFP 26-02-CD” in the subject line.
Proposal submissions must be received by the City Clerk's Office at the City of Destin, 4200 Indian Bayou
Trail, Destin, Florida 32541 in a sealed envelope clearly marked, RFP 26-02-CD, AI-Powered Development
Services Optimization Platform no later than 2:00 p.m. (CT) on April 9, 2026, along with the name,
return address and telephone number of the submitter. One printed copy of the proposal and one digital
copy shall be provided on a USB Drive (pdf format).
The City reserves the right to reject any and all proposals, waive minor defects and informalities, and accept
the proposal deemed in the best interest of the City.
Scope of Work
The City of Destin is issuing this Request for Proposals (RFP) to seek a qualified vendor to provide an AI-
assisted Development Services platform to improve regulatory compliance screening, enhance staff access
to municipal code knowledge, and support data-driven process improvements.
The scope of work includes, but is not limited to, the following:
1. AI Plan Review & Compliance Screening
The system shall:
•Perform automated compliance screening for residential and commercial development applications.
•Validate submissions against zoning districts, setbacks, parking standards, landscaping requirements, and
applicable LDC standards.
•Generate draft review comment letters with applicable code citations.
•Produce structured deficiency matrices.
•Provide recurring compliance trend and friction reporting.
•Integrate with the City’s existing permitting workflows and systems.
2.LDC & Ordinance Digitization
The vendor shall:
•Encode the City’s Land Development Code into machine-readable rulesets.
•Structure relevant ordinances and procedures into AI-accessible logic.
•Maintain version control reflecting future ordinance amendments.

• Provide structured update procedures when code changes occur.

The system shall include:

procedures.

4. GIS & Geospatial Integration (If Applicable)

• GIS-integrated validation of zoning and parcel-based standards.

5. Optional Modernization & Analytics Components

• Draft amendment support.
• Data-driven regulatory modernization recommendations.
6. Governance & Safeguards
The vendor must clearly describe:
• Human-in-the-loop review safeguards.
• Audit trail and public records compliance (Florida Statutes Chapter 119).
• Data security architecture.
• Confirmation that City data will not train public or external AI models.
• Role of City staff in maintaining final authority over regulatory interpretation.
7. Implementation & Deployment
Proposals shall include:
• Detailed implementation timeline (target 60–90 days full capability).
• Project management structure.
• Staff onboarding and training plan.

A complete contractor-generated invoice shall be provided electronically as an attachment via IPP.

The Contractor shall submit any other information or documentation required by other clauses of the Task Order to support the invoice. Original receipts shall be maintained by the Contractor and made available to Government auditors upon request.

Contractors shall comply with the government’s invoicing period, which runs from the first through the last day of the calendar month. Contractors shall invoice the government in arrears for every month services are provided, by the 15th day of the following month. All invoicing shall be rendered based on calendar month cycles.

Proration: When applicable, monthly charges will be prorated by the number of days the service was performed divided by the number of days in that month.

Rounding: all charges will be rounded to two (2) decimal places.

Reporting

The contractor shall comply with all contract reporting requirements.

Restrictions against Disclosure

The Contractor agrees, in the performance of this contract, to keep all information contained in source documents or other media furnished by the Government in the strictest confidence. The Contractor also agrees not to publish or otherwise divulge such information in whole or in part, in any manner or form, nor to authorize or permit others to do so, taking such reasonable measures as are necessary to restrict access to such information while in the Contractor’s possession, to those employees needing such information to perform the work provided herein, i.e., on a "need to know" basis. The Contractor agrees to immediately notify the Contracting Officer, in writing, in the event that the Contractor determines or has reason to suspect a breach of this requirement.

Dear GSA Schedule Contractor:
The purpose of this Request for Quotes (RFQ) is to acquire contractor support for a cadre of offices at the
Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB or the Bureau) to provide Design, Development, Content,
and Marketing (DDCM) Support Services. In accordance with the guidelines discussed below, CFPB
requests that you provide a Quote in response to this RFQ.
Pursuant to Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) Part 8, this is an RFQ issued as a 100% small business
set aside to selected vendors under GSA’s Multiple Award Schedule (MAS) SINs of 54151S, 541511,
541430, 541910, 541613 and 512110. Teaming and/or Subcontracting arrangements are highly
encouraged. CFPB also encourages the use of Minority and Women-Owned Businesses (MWOBs) in
such arrangements. FAR Parts 13, 14 and 15 are not applicable to this RFQ. There will be two pools
under this evaluation, under which CFPB intends to award multiple BPA(s): Design and Development
(Pool I: Task Areas 1.1-1.6) and Content and Marketing (Pool II: Task Areas 1.7-1.9). Vendors can
submit Quotes for one pool or both pools. Please note that all task areas must be addressed if vendors
submit Quotes for both pools, and all task areas within a pool must be addressed if a Quoter submits a
Quote for only one (1) particular pool. If all task areas are not addressed, the vendor/vendor team quote
shall be considered technically unacceptable.
Based upon its evaluation of Quotes received in response to this RFQ, CFPB intends to issue multiple
Blanket Purchase Agreements (BPAs) in each pool but reserves the right to issue one (1) BPA or no
BPAs under each pool. Detailed instructions for preparing and submitting a Quote are contained in the
RFQ, along with the criteria to be used by CFPB in evaluating Quotes.
This acquisition will be made using Best Value evaluation procedures, which may require a trade-off of
technical merit and price. The Government may issue one (1) or multiple BPAs resulting from this
solicitation to the responsible Offeror(s) whose Quote, conforming to the RFQ, offers fair and reasonable
pricing and represents the Best Value to the Government. See Section IV for specific details.

RFQ: 9531CB23Q0019
Design, Development, Content, and Marketing (DDCM) Support Services
SECTION A – Introduction
1.0 BACKGROUND.
The Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act of 2010 (“Dodd-Frank Act”), Pub. L.
111-203, established the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (“CFPB” or the “Bureau”) to regulate the
offering and provision of consumer products or services under federal consumer financial laws.
Consistent with the purposes of the Dodd-Frank Act, the CFPB’s mission is to establish and enforce clear,
consistent rules for the financial marketplace, protect American families from unfair financial practices,
and develop and implement a strategy to improve the financial literacy of consumers. For more
background on the CFPB, please see https://www.consumerfinance.gov/about-us/the-bureau/.
The Technology and Innovation (T&I) Office is responsible for delivering innovative technology
products and services for the Bureau. Integral to providing this support is to ensure all digital products
and services are fully developed, managed, operated, and maintained by highly qualified and skilled web
development and design resources. To be responsive to growing needs, the CFPB requires additional
resources to supplement its existing in-house capacity.
It is T&I’s objective, through this contractual requirement, to engage with Industry partners to acquire a
cadre of resources capable of providing a variety of web development and design support. The resulting
award(s) will enable the CFPB the flexibility to draw support as needed to meet not only time-sensitive
and specific requests, but also short- and long-term Bureau-wide projects.
CFPB makes use of modern best-practices and tools while taking a user-centered approach. This includes
using and contributing to open source, maintaining our design system, deploying to a modern cloud
environment, and conducting design sprints.
2.0 PURPOSE.
The purpose of this Blanket Purchase Agreement (BPA) competition is to obtain contractor support
services to provide web development, design, user research, multimedia, and programming support
services to the Technology and Innovation Team within the CFPB. The Contractor shall work with the
CFPB web development and design teams to assist with digital product development and iteration using a
variety of development technologies and resources.

RFQ: 9531CB23Q0019
Design, Development, Content, and Marketing (DDCM) Support Services
Section B- Scope and Tasks
1.0 SCOPE AND ANTICIPATED TASKS.
Contractors participating on this BPA shall provide support to the CFPB Design & Development Team to
develop digital products and services that communicate the CFPB's key messages clearly and effectively.
The scope will include the following task areas and their objectives (as well as the appropriate GSA MAS
SIN the task area corresponds to):
Information Technology (IT) Task Areas (1.1-1.6) Evaluation Pool I
1.1 Web development (SIN 54151S)
• Create web applications and web services using various web programming languages including
Python, Django, and JavaScript, and do so with a strong commitment to strengthening CFPB’s
existing platforms.
• Provide software development, systems administration, software quality, data management, data
analysis, and systems documentation support to the CFPB’s digital assets, including the Bureau’s
public-facing web site, consumerfinance.gov, as well as internal software tools.
• Support section 508 compliance and other web based specialized services.
1.2 Software delivery and DevOps (SIN 54151S)
• Deliver software to a containerized cloud environment, with a focus on optimization,
troubleshooting, and monitoring. Work with technical staff to develop technical documentation.
• Utilize Infrastructure as Code (IaC) to develop, deploy, and maintain environments and services
with Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) providers (e.g., Amazon Web Services, Microsoft Azure,
Google Compute Engine).
• Build and maintain CI/CD pipelines, tooling, and processes to support development, deployment,
and monitoring.
• Develop, deploy, maintain, and troubleshoot containerization technologies such as Docker and
Kubernetes.
1.3 Application Security Engineering (SIN 54151S)
• Configure, operate, maintain, and monitor various application security tools and services.
• Collaborate, champion, and mentor software development teams and other CFPB stakeholders on
secure software development, delivery, and operations.
1.4 Design (SINs 54151S/541511)
• Provide expertise in graphic design, user experience (UX) design, and product design, especially
as applies to digital products, to build valuable tools that benefit the CFPB’s day-to-day
operations and broader mission to serve the public.
• Facilitate design thinking workshops to facilitate brainstorming, problem statement development,
and design exploration.
1.5 User research and usability testing (SINs 541511/541910)

See Attachment 5 for a sample Quality Assurance Surveillance Plan (QASP). A QASP will be included
with awarded BPA Call and will be pursuant to the requirements listed in the Performance Work
Statement (PWS) of each Call. This performance-based plan sets forth the procedures and guidelines
CFPB will use in evaluating the technical performance of the Contractor. The QASP does not detail how
the Contractor accomplishes the work; however, it provides the method and basis for the Contracting
Officer’s Representative (COR) to evaluate the quality of the Contractor’s performance.
5.0 INSPECTION AND ACCEPTANCE.
Inspection and acceptance of all products and services shall be by the COR and ACOR. Deliverables and
services shall be reviewed for accuracy, clarity, completeness, and timeliness within five working days after
receipt unless specified differently elsewhere in the contract. CFPB will provide any concerns and
comments to the Contractor, who shall ensure completion of each deliverable in a manner acceptable to the
COR and ACOR within three working days of such CFPB notice. The Contractor shall deliver all copies
of completed deliverables to the COR and ACOR.
6.0 EXTENT OF OBLIGATION
The Government is obligated under this BPA only to the extent of the authorized Calls issued by a CO.
This BPA does not obligate any funds.
7.0 PERIOD OF PERFORMANCE.

5.0 ISSUANCE OF BPA CALLS.
5.1 The Government will order services through the issuance of Calls on a Firm Fixed Price,
Time & Materials, or Labor Hour basis, or a hybrid thereof. Each Call will have a unique
identifier and will obligate funds (incrementally or in total) to cover the work required
under that Call. In no event shall work performed under a Call exceed any specified price
ceiling in that Call. Under multiple BPA awards, Calls will be issued competitively
whenever feasible using the Call Ordering Procedures detailed below, unless otherwise
specified in the relevant RFQ.
5.2 All work requested, work performed, and performance periods shall be within the scope of
work identified within the BPA(s). In the event that a Call requires performance that
extends beyond the current BPA performance period, the Contractor shall be required to
complete performance within the period set forth in the Call, provided such period does
not extend beyond one (1) year after the expiration of the BPA.
6.0 CALL ORDERING PROCEDURES.
6.1 GENERAL.
• The Government may order up to the maximum order limitation specified in the
vendor’s relevant GSA Schedule. The Government will seek additional discounts
for Calls above the maximum order limitation specified in the vendor’s relevant
GSA Schedule. However, all Calls are subject to the terms and conditions of the
BPA. In the event of a conflict between a Call and the BPA, the BPA shall prevail.
The Contractor shall not commence work until authorized by the Contracting
Officer through the issuance of a BPA Call.
• Under multiple BPAs, Calls will be issued on a best value basis, considering price
and nonprice factors; the importance of non-price factors versus price will be
expressly stated in the Request for Quotation. If expressly stated, a Call may be
issued on a Lowest-Priced Technically Acceptable (LPTA) basis when such basis
provides the best value to the Government.
• Calls under single-award BPAs will be evaluated for technical acceptability and
price reasonableness.
6.2 ORDERING PROCEDURES.
• Prior to task order issuance, the Contracting Officer will issue a Request for
Quotation (RFQ) to all Contractors unless otherwise specified in the RFQ,
identifying the type of Call and the deadline for submission of the quote.

in paragraph (d)(2) of this provision if the Offeror has represented that it "does not use covered

(a) Definitions. As used in this provision—
Backhaul, covered telecommunications equipment or services, critical technology, interconnection
arrangements, reasonable inquiry, roaming, and substantial or essential component have the meanings
provided in the clause 52.204-25, Prohibition on Contracting for Certain Telecommunications and Video
Surveillance Services or Equipment.
(b) Prohibition.
(1) Section 889(a)(1)(A) of the John S. McCain National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2019
(Pub. L. 115-232) prohibits the head of an executive agency on or after August 13, 2019, from procuring
or obtaining, or extending or renewing a contract to procure or obtain, any equipment, system, or service
that uses covered telecommunications equipment or services as a substantial or essential component of
any system, or as critical technology as part of any system. Nothing in the prohibition shall be construed
to—
(i) Prohibit the head of an executive agency from procuring with an entity to provide a service
that connects to the facilities of a third-party, such as backhaul, roaming, or interconnection
arrangements; or
(ii) Cover telecommunications equipment that cannot route or redirect user data traffic or
cannot permit visibility into any user data or packets that such equipment transmits or otherwise handles.
(2) Section 889(a)(1)(B) of the John S. McCain National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal
Year 2019 (Pub. L. 115-232) prohibits the head of an executive agency on or after August 13, 2020, from
entering into a contract or extending or renewing a contract with an entity that uses any equipment,
system, or service that uses covered telecommunications equipment or services as a substantial or
essential component of any system, or as critical technology as part of any system. This prohibition
applies to the use of covered telecommunications equipment or services, regardless of whether that use is
in performance of work under a Federal contract. Nothing in the prohibition shall be construed to—

a) Technical Approach. The Government will assess its level of confidence that

b) Staffing Plan. The Government will assess its level of confidence that the

4.2 Total Evaluated Price Factor. To be considered for award, the Quoter must submit
prices which comply with the Pricing Template provided in Attachment 2. Quoters who
fail to comply with these requirements will receive no further consideration and may be
eliminated from this evaluation. The Government will evaluate the completed Pricing
Template (Attachment 2) for the purposes of determining whether the proposed Total
Evaluated Price is fair and reasonable. Quoters’ proposed Total Evaluated Prices will be
used for evaluation purposes only.
4.3 Discussions. The Government intends to make an award on the basis of initial quotes
without discussions. Therefore, each Quoter shall make its single best effort in its initial
technical and pricing quotes. However, if after evaluating the initial quotes, the
Government finds that discussions are required or otherwise in its best interest, it may
conduct them with all or a limited number of the Quoters.
4.4 Award Determination. Award will be made as follows:
(1) Best Value Trade Off. While the Government is concerned both with obtaining
superior performance, and obtaining a fair and reasonable price, the two (2) non-
price factors, when combined, are significantly more important than price. The
Government will evaluate each evaluation pool separately and determine which
quote(s) represent the Best Value for each pool. Quoters may be issued a BPA
under one pool or both pools.
4.5 Comparative Evaluation. The Government may perform a comparative evaluation
(comparing offers to each other) to select the quotes that are best suited and provides the
best value in each pool, considering the evaluation factors in this solicitation.
4.6 Total Evaluated Price. To be considered for award, the Quoter must submit pricing in
accordance with Attachment 2. Quoters who fail to comply with these requirements will
receive no further consideration and may be eliminated from this evaluation. The
Government will assess the reasonableness of each proposed Total Evaluated Price.
4.7 Award on Initial Responses. The Government anticipates selecting the best-suited
quoter(s) from initial responses, without engaging in exchanges with quoters. Quoters are
strongly encouraged to submit their best technical solutions and price in response to this
RFQ.

provided; and explanation of the proposed use of covered telecommunications services and any factors

provision.

following information as part of the offer:

name, unique entity identifier, CAGE code, and whether the entity was the OEM or a distributor, if
known);
(B) A description of all covered telecommunications equipment offered (include brand;
model number, such as OEM number, manufacturer part number, or wholesaler number; and item
description, as applicable); and
(C) Explanation of the proposed use of covered telecommunications equipment and any
factors relevant to determining if such use would be permissible under the prohibition in paragraph (b)(2)
of this provision.
(ii) For covered services—
(A) If the service is related to item maintenance: A description of all covered
telecommunications services offered (include on the item being maintained: Brand; model number, such
as OEM number, manufacturer part number, or wholesaler number; and item description, as applicable);
or
(B) If not associated with maintenance, the PSC of the service being provided; and
explanation of the proposed use of covered telecommunications services and any factors relevant to
determining if such use would be permissible under the prohibition in paragraph (b)(2) of this provision.
(End of provision)

FLORIDA VIRTUAL SCHOOL
REQUEST FOR PROPOSAL
Information Technology Projects: Full-Service Software Development Firms
1. OVERVIEW
1.1. Purpose. Florida Virtual School (FLVS) is seeking proposals from qualified vendors to deliver full-
service software development for enhancements to existing applications and to develop new
applications. The selected vendor(s) will be responsible for the complete software development
lifecycle (SDLC), including project management, business analysis, development, quality
assurance, deployment, documentation, and post-launch support. Respondents must be able to
fully manage and deliver secure, scalable, and user-friendly applications that meet FLVS’s
business and technical requirements, align with industry’s best practices, and support future
enhancements. FLVS seeks qualified proposers with the ability to develop independently a variety
of IT development initiatives that will vary in duration and scope.
1.2. About FLVS
Founded in 1997, Florida Virtual School (FLVS) has been leading the way in Kindergarten-12
online education for more than 25 years. FLVS provides a robust, award-winning curriculum to
public, private, charter, and homeschool families and school districts nationwide. FLVS offers a
variety of educational products and services to B2B and B2C audiences, including: K-12 Online
Schools, Teacher Training and Professional Development, Digital Curriculum (190+ Courses),
Tech Solutions and Platforms. Additional information about FLVS can be found
on https://www.flexpointeducation.com/ (National Products and Services) and www.flvs.net
(Florida Services).
FLVS directly serves students through three models. FLVS Flex which offers scheduling on a
365-day flexible calendar. Students can take FLVS courses when enrolled in addition to a brick-
and-mortar school, or a full-time course load when enrolled as a homeschool student. FLVS Full
Time Public Schools offer scheduling that is available to students in grades Kindergarten–12.
Students follow a 180-day traditional school calendar, take all courses through FLVS Full Time,
and graduate with an FLVS diploma. FLVS is the operating education service provider for the
Florida Scholars Academy, a unified education system for students assigned to a Florida
Department of Juvenile Justice residential commitment program. Across these three programs,
FLVS serves over 250,000 students a year.
1.3. Contract Scope, Structure, Terms, and Pricing
1.3.1. Contract Services, Structure, Term, Sequence and Pricing: FLVS shall enter into a
“single contract” with a “single prime Proposer”, or multiple proposers as deemed to be in
FLVS’s best interest for all services as described under this RFP. It is the responsibility
of the Proposer to partner as necessary and assemble the team, skills, assets and other
qualifications to meet all requirements in the RFP. The selected Proposer shall assume
all contractual responsibilities and comply with all contract terms and conditions.
1.3.2. Scope of Work: The scope of work under this contract is in Section 5 of this RFP.
1.3.3 Contract Structure: FLVS shall contract the services described herein under a single
contract. The single contract, however, shall be packaged into one Master Service
Agreement (“MSA”) with an associated Scope of Work (SOW). A separate SOW shall be

Florida Virtual School Procurement Opportunities: https://www.flvs.net/about/procurement
2.3 Reply Opening. All submissions will be opened by FLVS Procurement via Zoom Conference
as per the cover sheet. The list of responses received shall be made available within 48 hours
following the opening as described in 2.2 Public Notices.
2.4 Responsiveness to Submittal Requirements. A preliminary evaluation by FLVS Procurement
staff shall determine whether each response received prior to the due date and time specified, is
complete and compliant with the instructions and requirements specified in the RFP. Findings
shall be reported to the PEC during the evaluation session. Any responses that are incomplete
or that do not comply with the instructions and /or submittal terms and conditions may be rejected
and excluded from further consideration. Responses which are compliant as determined solely
by FLVS, are moved to the evaluation stage.
2.5 Proposal Evaluation
2.5.1 Step 1: Evaluation. Proposals will be evaluated and scored by a PEC approved by the
Procurement Director or designee using the evaluation criteria specified in Section 2.6.1.
Step 1a: The PEC will discuss all contractor responses, and each evaluation committee
member will score the responses individually.
Step 1b: Procurement will combine the evaluation scores submitted by all PEC members
and shall determine the highest scoring responses based on the total evaluation scores
received. PEC shall determine the cutoff score and the number of responses that may
advance to Step 2. Step 2 is optional at the sole discretion of FLVS. If FLVS determines
that Step 2 is not required, award recommendation will be made based on the scores from
Step 1.
2.5.2 Step 2 (optional): Presentation and/or Interview. Finalists shall be notified by FLVS
Procurement to appear in-person or via Zoom meeting at a designated date and time for further
detailed discussions of the elements of their response. Step 2 evaluation is a “fresh evaluation”
with scores from step 1 not carried forward.
a. Using the evaluation criteria in Section 2.6.2, each member of the PEC will separately
score each respondent’s demonstration/oral presentation.
b. The procurement representative will combine the evaluation scores submitted by all PEC
members for this step to determine the final rankings.
c. FLVS at its sole discretion may require additional public meeting(s).
2.6 Evaluation Criteria. The responsive replies will be evaluated and ranked on a scale of 0-10 using
the following criteria multiplied by the weights assigned. This meeting shall be open to the public
via the zoom link provided on the cover page.
2.6.1 Proposal Evaluation Criteria Step 1. These criteria shall be used through the evaluation
process as described:
Criteria # Step 1: Main Criteria Description Weight
1. Qualifications, Experience, Letters of Reference, and Case 45%
Studies
2. Demonstrated ability to meet or exceed the Scope of Services 35%
and Responses to Questionnaire
3. Cost Proposal 20%
TOTAL 100%

3.2.5 Financial Stability. Financial stability means, at a minimum, having adequate income
and capital and the capacity to efficiently allocate resources, assess, and manage financial risks,
and maintain financial soundness through the term of the Agreement. Bidder/Proposer shall
demonstrate financial stability in accordance with this definition by providing one of the following:
1. For bidder/proposer with annual revenues below $1 billion:
• Audited financial statements that demonstrate their satisfaction of financial stability criteria
or;
• Documentation of an investment grade rating from a credit reporting agency designated
as a nationally recognized statistical rating organization by the Securities and Exchange
Commission.
2. In addition to the above two options, bidders/proposers with annual revenues exceeding $1
billion can provide a letter containing a written declaration, pursuant to s.92.525, F.S., issued
by the chief financial officer or controller attesting that the supplier is financially stable and
meets the definition of financial stability.
3.2.6 Accessibility Requirements. Respondent deliverables shall adhere with Americans
with Disabilities Act (ADA) in accordance with federal, state and local disability rights legislation
in accordance with WCAG 2.1 and 508 Accessibility Standards. Respondents shall also be able
to provide an accessibility conformance report using the Voluntary Product Accessibility Template
(VPAT) VPAT 2.5 WCAG which can be found at https://www.itic.org/policy/accessibility/vpat.
3.3 Proposal Section 2 – Qualifications, Background and References Instructions. Summarize
the qualifications of the Proposer’s project team. Where the project team includes sub-contractors
or sub-consultants, qualifications of the proposed sub-contractors or sub-consultants shall also
be provided.
3.3.1 Background (Qualifications). Complete the Compliance Information Sheet within the
forms packet Attachment 1.
3.3.2 Experience. Describe your company’s experience with the services specified herein.
Include any strategic advantages offered, and/or professional/industry awards won over the past
three years (URLs acceptable).
3.3.3 References. FLVS reserves the right to contact all references and to obtain, without
limitation, information on the Proposer’s performance on the listed efforts.
a. Provide three (3) written letters of reference from the last thirty-six (36) months. Letters
of reference should be on company letterhead from the referee and include signature and
contact information.
b. Proposers are required to sign the Authorization for (Appendix G) to contact and check
previous performance on projects.
3.3.4 Case Studies and Supplier Qualifications. Proposer shall include a minimum of three
(3) Case Studies demonstrating prior experience and proven success delivering similar services
to other clients. Proposers shall focus on highlighting the supplier qualifications listed in
the table below.
a. Describe the client requirements, challenges, budget, goals, and objectives.
b. Strategies developed to achieve goals and objectives.
c. Explain in detail what the outcome was and what value your services realized for the
client against chosen metrics.

5. SCOPE OF WORK
Scope of Services: Florida Virtual School (FLVS) is seeking proposals from qualified vendors to deliver full-service
software development for enhancements to existing applications and new applications. The selected vendor will be
responsible for the complete software development lifecycle (SDLC), including project management, business
analysis, development, quality assurance, deployment, documentation, and post-launch support. Respondents
must be able to fully manage and deliver secure, scalable, and user-friendly applications that meet FLVS’s business
and technical requirements, align with industry’s best practices, and support future enhancements. FLVS is not
seeking a supplier to provide resources to supplement internal development efforts.
Sample and not all-inclusive list of minimum Delivery requirements for all potential projects.
• Requirements documentation and user stories
• Technical architecture and design artifacts
• Fully developed and tested application
• Production deployment and environment setup
• End-user and technical documentation
• Training sessions and knowledge transfer
• Warranty support and optional maintenance plan
All awarded proposers will have to meet the above minimum delivery requirements for all projects as well as any
individual project delivery requirements.
The Respondent is responsible for providing appropriate work environment including high speed internet
connectivity to appointed staff for the duration of this project. Reimbursement for travel is not contemplated for
this RFP. However, in the rare event that FLVS directly requests and agrees to reimburse any travel related costs
they must be authorized and pre-approved by FLVS in writing and will be reimbursed in accordance with applicable
Florida Statute and FLVS Policy.
Awarded Contracts. FLVS will issue Requests for Quote(s) (RFQs) to contractors awarded under this RFP for IT
to deliver full-service software development Project Services. RFQs, which may result in the issuance of an SOW
detailing the specific services or projects to be performed by the selected Contractor, shall also be set forth in the
contract or purchase order. Pursuant to section 287.056(2), F.S., RFQs performed within the scope of this RFP
are not independent competitive solicitations and are not subject to the notice or challenge provisions of section
120.57(3), F.S.
FLVS shall obtain quotes from all qualified awarded Contractors and, at its sole discretion;
a) award to the lowest-priced quote
b) award based upon evaluation of criteria specified to the highest-ranked response to the
RFQ
FLVS at its sole discretion may choose to compete any requirement regardless of value at its sole discretion as in
its best interest. Projects may be quoted and awarded at our discretion using time and materials or on a fixed fee
basis. FLVS reserves the right to make award(s) by individual item, group of items, all or none or a combination
thereof; to reject any and/or all bids/proposals or to waive any minor irregularity or technicality. Responders are
cautioned to make no assumptions unless their proposal has been evaluated as being responsive. Cash discounts
for prompt payment shall not be considered in determining the lowest net cost for bid/proposal evaluation
purposes.

4. INSTRUCTIONS TO PROPOSER
4.1 No Contact Period. To ensure proper and fair evaluation, FLVS has established a Cone of
Silence applicable to all Competitive Solicitations. The Cone of Silence is designed to protect the
integrity of the procurement process by shielding it from undue influences. The Cone of Silence
will be imposed upon all Competitive Solicitations (including the RFP) beginning with the
advertisement for the same and ending with the approval for award.
Upon the issuance of this RFP, all contact with FLVS must be made through the
procurement representative named on the first page. The Proposer must limit communication
with the designated contact to the means specified in this document. Other employees and
representatives of FLVS and the participating agencies are instructed not to answer questions
regarding the RFP or otherwise discuss the contents of the RFP with the Proposer or its
representatives. Any contact made with other employees and representatives of FLVS will be
reported and forwarded to Procurement. Proposer shall not, under the penalty of law, offer any
gratuities, favors or anything of monetary value to any officer or employee of FLVS in connection
with this competitive procurement.
4.2 Proposers or persons acting on their behalf are specifically instructed not to contact Board of
Trustees, members, staff, or Committee Members during the course of the Response and
Selection process. All procedural matters shall be directed to Procurement. Evaluation Committee
members or other School employees shall not be contacted or approached by representatives of
any potential Proposer to this RFP. Contact or communication initiated by any responding firm
may result in rejection of the Response.
4.3 Public Records. Upon award recommendation or thirty (30) days after opening, whichever is
earlier, any material submitted in response to this RFP will become a public record and shall be
subject to public disclosure consistent with Chapter 119, Florida Statutes (Public Records Law).
Proposers must claim the applicable exemptions to disclosure provided by law, in their response
to the RFP, by identifying materials to be protected and must state the reasons why such exclusion
from public disclosure is necessary and legal. FLVS reserves the right to make any final
determination of the applicability of the Public Records Law.
4.4 Posting of Public Notices: All public notices will be posted for review by interested parties on
https://flvs.bonfirehub.com and https://www.flvs.net/about/procurement. Notices will remain
posted for a period of 72 hours. Failure to file a Notice of Intent to Protest within the time prescribed
in Section 120.57(3) b, Florida Statutes, shall constitute a waiver of proceedings under Chapter
120, Florida Statutes.
4.5 Bid Protests: Any person who believes they are adversely affected by any specification in this
Bid or RFP or any decision or intended decision concerning this Bid or RFP and who wishes to
protest such specification, decision, or intended decision shall file a Notice of Intent to Protest in
accordance with Section 120.57(3), Florida Statutes. Notice must be sent to
procurement@flvs.net. Following Notice, a formal written protest must be accompanied by a bond
payable to FLVS in an amount equal to one percent (1%) of the total value of the proposed
contract. Security shall be in the form of a bond, a cashier’s check, or money order. Failure to file
a protest within the time prescribed in Section 120.57(3), Florida Statutes, or failure to post the
bond or other security within the time frame set forth in Section 287.042(2)(c), Florida Statutes,
shall constitute a waiver of proceedings under Chapter 120, Florida Statutes.
4.6 Written Clarifications: Any Proposer in doubt as to the true meaning of any part of this RFP or
related documents must submit a written request for clarification through Bonfire no later than the
date and time specified on the cover sheet. Any interpretation to a Proposer shall be made only

2.2 AGENCY BACKGROUND
The DPS NCEM (North Carolina Division of Emergency Management) was established to enhance the
state's resiliency by actively collaborating, communicating, and coordinating to prevent, mitigate, respond,
and recover from disasters. NCEM deploys state resources when needed, and coordinates with neighboring
states and the federal government to augment staffing and resources. The division also administers state
and federal grants, manages multi-agency response to disasters, oversees all hazards and threat risk
management, coordinates regional hazard mitigation plans, facilitates trainings and exercises, and manages
assets such as the regional hazmat response and search-and-rescue teams. Additionally, NCEM develops
and maintains flood maps for each county in NC and maintains the official survey database for the state and
manages the State’s Homeland Security program.
.
2.3 SUMMARY OF PROBLEM STATEMENT
The current EPR Plan Template Solution faces several limitations, including outdated technology, technical
issues with NCID authentication, significant barriers for user access, and poor data management as
demonstrated by redundant, duplicated, or obsolete emergency plans.
• Outdated Technology: The current technology stack is over 10 years old and does not meet the
Agency’s current technology standards.
• User Access: NCID authentication does not meet current business needs because DIT policy requires
disabling inactive NCID accounts every 90 days. This leaves infrequent users without a way to access
the system, creating additional work on support staff to provision access. The project will replace NCID
authentication with a vendor implemented authentication solution designed and operated in alignment
with the password complexity and authentication requirements outlined in the North Carolina Statewide
Information Security Manual (SISM).
• Outdated Content: The emergency management plans need updated content. There are sections of the
plan missing the latest training information. The process for handling staff content changes needs to be
significantly improved. The ability to designate content authors is needed, as well as the ability for authors
to designate contributors or co-authors.
• Data Management: There are more than 12,000 plans in the system. Considering there are only 5,325
childcare facilities, on average up to 15-20 duplicate plans are created for a single facility. The excessive
number of plans per facility creates confusion because it is difficult to locate the latest plan. Designing a
repeatable business process for creating and managing plans will reduce issues with data duplication,
data redundancy and versioning.

4.0 COST OF VENDOR’S OFFER
4.1 OFFER COSTS
The Vendor must list, itemize, and describe any applicable offer costs in ATTACHMENT D: COST
FORM.
4.2 PAYMENT SCHEDULE
The Vendor shall propose its itemized payment schedule based on the content of its offer. All
payments must be based upon acceptance of one or more Deliverables.
5.0 EVALUATION
5.1 SOURCE SELECTION
A trade-off/ranking method of source selection will be utilized in this procurement to allow the State
to award this RFP to the Vendor providing the Best Value and recognizing that Best Value may result
in award other than the lowest price or highest technically qualified offer. By using this method, the
overall ranking may be adjusted up or down when considered with or traded-off against other non-
price factors.
a) Evaluation Process Explanation. State Agency employees will review all offers. All offers will be
initially classified as being responsive or non-responsive. If an offer is found non-responsive, it
will not be considered further. All responsive offers will be evaluated based on stated evaluation
criteria. Any references in an answer to another location in the RFP materials or Offer shall have
specific page numbers and sections stated in the reference.
b) To be eligible for consideration, Vendor’s offer must substantially conform to the intent of all
specifications. Compliance with the intent of all specifications will be determined by the State.
Offers that do not meet the full intent of all specifications listed in this RFP may be deemed
deficient. Further, a serious deficiency in the offer to any one (1) factor may be grounds for
rejection regardless of overall score.
c) The evaluation committee may request clarifications, an interview with or presentation from any
or all Vendors as allowed by 9 NCAC 06B.0307. However, the State may refuse to accept, in full
