14. Availability and Resilience
The Third Party is expected to make their services available with a minimum of 99.9% uptime or greater
at agreed-upon service levels. NYCPS expects the Third Party to continue to provide services in the event
of disruption with a quick turnaround time. The availability and resiliency must be according to the best
practices (e.g., Business Continuity Plan / Disaster Recovery), security controls and procedures adopted
by the Third Party. The Third Party must ensure a repeatable process to address the Availability and
Resilience control section. This may include supporting policies, standards, and Procedures.
Ref. Requirements
14.1 The Third-Party Systems that handle Confidential Information must be available and fully
functional 24x7x365 with 99.9% uptime. The Third Party must make plans for colocation,
backups, and any other Systems necessary to ensure continuity.
14.2 Third Party recovery plans must have recovery strategies in place to adequately address common
disruption scenarios, in accordance with system criticality and scale of end-user population.
14.3 The Third Party should ensure recovery plans are updated, reviewed, and approved by the Third
Party at least annually or as material changes occur within their operating environment.
14.4 The Third Party must be able to take action to implement measures that will enable it to conduct
testing in conditions comparable to production and demonstrate recovery within the established
Recovery Time Objectives (RTO) or on a timely manner.
The Third Party must consider the following during a Disaster Recovery situation:
- Ensure the changes affecting recovery of processes are identified and tested.
- All applications employ a backup policy as a fallback mechanism.
- Procedures in place to enable recovery of internal IT services to normal operation.
- Recovery plans to be ready for any significant change (such as change in personnel, etc.).
14.5 The Third Party must have a crisis management framework including issue identification, issue
escalation, and notification of NYCPS in the event a breach has occurred.
14.6 NYCPS Confidential Information must be available upon request, in an industry-standard format,
so as to ensure portability and interoperability.
14.7 If the Third Party provides a SaaS service, it must provide geographically resilient hosting options.
The Third Party must have more than one provider for each service for which there is a service
delivery dependency (availability zone within the USA region).

15. Technology Operations (Management)
The Third Party must ensure their products and services to NYCPS perform as required, by adopting best
practices (e.g., change management, performance management), security controls and procedures for
maintaining service levels and well-defined processes in place. The Third Party must ensure a repeatable
process to address the Technology Operations (Management) control section. This may include
supporting policies, standards, and procedures.
Ref. Requirements
15.1 Third Party should have documented operational procedures to ensure the correct and secure
operation of assets.
15.2 Operational procedures must include monitoring of capacity and performance.
15.3 Third Party should ensure changes to the production system, network, applications, data file
structures, other system components, and physical/environment be monitored and controlled
through a formal, documented change control process.
15.4 Third Party should ensure changes be tested prior to implementation and reviewed for impact.
15.5 Changes must be approved by the Third Party prior to implementation, and evaluated after
implementation to ensure that the expected outcome was achieved.
15.6 Third Party should ensure an emergency change management procedure be documented.
15.7 The Third Party should ensure that all infrastructure assets follow a documented and approved
technology maintenance process. Additionally, Third Parties should ensure regular maintenance of
and updates to infrastructure assets, as well as regular testing to verify the effectiveness of these
updates.
V1.8 [Public] NYC Public Schools Third Party Information Security Requirements page 18

16. Risk Management and Compliance
The Third Party must conduct a risk assessment and compliance exercise with regard to products/services
supplied to NYCPS to identify potential risks and compliance issues, reduce exposure, and improve their
overall risk management and compliance processes. The Third Party must ensure a repeatable process to
address the Risk Management and Compliance control section. This may include supporting policies,
standards, and procedures.
Ref. Requirements
16.1 Third Party should regularly validate the effectiveness of controls through a risk assessment program
and appropriately manage remediation efforts.
16.2 The Third Party should conduct a risk assessment biannually and plan to address any discovered
issues and verify the implementation of controls that protect business operations and NYCPS
Confidential Information.
16.3 Third Party should have a risk-based exception management process in place for prioritization
and remediation or risk acceptance of controls that have not been adopted or implemented.

17. Physical and Environmental Security
The Third Party must implement physical and environmental safety measures to reduce hazards to
human safety, the risk of data theft, data loss, and damage to assets during transfer. The Third Party
must ensure a repeatable process to address the Physical and Environmental Security control section.
This may include supporting policies, standards, and procedures.
Ref. Requirements
17.1 Physical and environmental security processes and procedures must be in place for facilities with
access to, or storage of, NYCPS Confidential Information.
17.2 Any stored data must be monitored using detective monitoring controls (e.g., CCTV) for a defined
retention period.
17.3 The Third Party must ensure that facilities maintain appropriate environmental controls to protect
against environmental hazards. Third Party must establish and implement procedures for monitoring
and maintaining fire detection and suppression systems, climate control and monitoring systems,
power and backup power solutions, and water damage detection systems.
17.4 Third Party should have a sufficient technology asset registration policy and procedure that includes
unique identifiers for all assets, the appropriate classification of assets, designation of asset ownership,
asset location, and proper licensing to meet all legal, regulatory, contractual, or support requirements.
17.5 A technology asset provisioning and disposal program must be in place to ensure procurement of
technology assets from appropriately sourced suppliers and disposal/removal/deletion of all
technology assets in a secure manner when they reach end of life (EOL).
17.6 The Third Party must ensure assets are transported in a secure manner.

4. Authentication and Identity Management
The Third Party must ensure a repeatable process to address the Authentication and Identity
Management control section. This may include supporting policies, standards, and procedures.
Ref. Requirements
4.1 The Third Party must create access controls that implement role-based, "need-to-know" access
according to the principle of least privilege.
4.2 The Third Party should have a policy and supporting controls in place for remote access.
Additionally, the Third Party should manage remote access controls and require periodic
recertification. The Remote access policy should include language related to security end point
device (Poster check) and restriction of access such as blocking split tunneling.
4.3 The Third Party must adhere to user provisioning and password complexity consistent with
commonly accepted practices.
4.4 Access rights for all employees, consultants and users to information and processing facilities must
be adjusted upon change of roles, or removed immediately upon the termination of employment,
contract, or agreement.
4.5 The Third Party should support single sign-on authentication for NYCPS. Users using either OpenID
Security Assertion Markup Language (SAML) 2.0, OAuth 2.0 or the current non-depreciated
equivalent. NYCPS now also supports Learning Tools Interoperability (LTI).
Note: Third Party will not have the ability to make any changes to the NYCPS Identity Management
Systems.
4.6 If the Third Party maintains its own identity management system for its users, the Third Party
should abide by established guidelines and follow best practices to ensure the security of user
accounts, access provisioning, escalation of privileges and timely termination. Below are some
examples, including but not limited to:
(a) do not hardcode credentials,
(b) use a password Reset tool whenever necessary,
(c) implement account lockout against brute force attack,
(d) do not disclose critical information in error messages,
(e) encrypt credentials in transit,
(f) set a password expiry routine where required.
4.7 Two-factor authentication must be implemented for all privileged access sessions.

10. Application Development
The Third Party must ensure that they adhere to secure coding standards, security testing, and
penetration testing, where applicable, with appropriate policies, standards, set of controls and
procedures consistent with best practices. The Third Party must ensure a repeatable process to address
the Application Development control section. This may include supporting policies, standards, and
procedures.
Ref. Requirements
10.1 Third Party should have a comprehensive secure development lifecycle system in place
consistent with industry standard best practices, including policies, training, audits, testing,
emergency updates, proactive management, and regular updates to the secure development
lifecycle system itself. (e.g., OWASP Top 10 to identify vulnerabilities / design security flaws).
10.2 Third Parties that handle Confidential Information should respond to and resolve security-related
bug reports, inquiries, and incidents in a timely and professional manner.
10.3 Software and firmware components that Third Parties provide should undergo a Static Application
Security Test (SAST) to ensure provided source code does not have any known security issues and
that all critical and high vulnerabilities have been remediated in the product version that NYCPS
will purchase. Note: this can be done on the source code or binaries.
10.4 The Third Party must conduct penetration testing as necessary and maintain proper oversight of
their controls.
10.5 Annual penetration tests must include all Systems exposed to the internet and any Systems,
internal or external, that handle Confidential Information.
10.6 All web application screens that contain Confidential Information must be non-cacheable.
10.7 Confidential Information must not appear in URLs.
10.8 Third Parties’ development, Test and QA environments must not use Confidential Information
unless additional safeguards (e.g., masking) are put into place to protect Confidential Information.

9. System Configuration & Maintenance
The Third Party must adhere to best practices, standards, security controls to ensure system security,
secure data exchanges, reliability, and performance such as configuration management, change
management, backup, and recovery routines. The Third Party must ensure a repeatable process to
address the System Configuration & Maintenance control section. This may include supporting policies,
standards, and procedures.
Ref. Requirements
9.1 All operating systems, servers, and network devices that store Confidential Information must be
kept hardened and patched. Standard security configurations, using the principles of least
functionality/privileges, must be established.
9.2 All Third-Party systems that are used to host, transfer, or otherwise interact with Confidential
Information must enforce strict separation from any non-NYCPS Systems. This may be achieved
through physical and/or logical separation. The separation must be auditable and able to be
proven at the request of the NYCPS.
9.3 The Third Party must maintain technical best security practices configuration guidelines for all
systems and update them at least twice per year.
9.4 Third Party should ensure that their current network diagram, including devices, as well as a data
flow diagram is maintained and frequently updated to ensure that they accurately reflect the
current state of the network and data environment.
9.5 Third Party should ensure deviations from hardened builds/security configuration baselines are
identified and remediated.
9.6 The Third Party must ensure that malware protection mechanisms are in place to detect and
prevent malware and other threats.
9.7 The Third Party must have controls that ensure proper control of an electronic mail and/or
instant messaging system that displays and/or contains NYCPS information.
9.8 The Third Party must ensure that preventive controls are in place to block malicious messages
and attachments and prevent the auto-forwarding of emails.
9.9 The Third Party must have a procedure for conducting digital forensics including data collection,
data/evidence preservation for future analysis, reporting of findings, and closure to respond to
adverse events.
9.10 Third Party should have a process in place to conduct walk-through / tabletop simulation
exercises with appropriate reporting, remediation/acceptance, and tracking of findings.

*This should be the latest version available at the time of development.
Authentication
New applications should integrate with DOE SSO, IBM Security Access Manager (ISAM). ISAM supports the OAuth 2.0
protocol and OpenID Connect, Enable MFA (Multi Factor Authentication).
Authorization
Use Department of Education Role Based Access Control (RBAC) to restrict access to authorized users.
Application Architecture
The architecture of the application should be extendable from the front-end, the back-end and from the design
perspective for the changing needs of the business.
New application should be developed using latest designs, layered architecture (Domain, Repository, Services,
Infrastructure and UI), Micro Services, Cloud Native Architecture), application should be stateless.
Analytics & Reporting
Application should provide advanced reporting capabilities, ability for users to generate reports.
a. Reports and analytics should be generated from read only Always-ON replica of the databases.
b. Reporting capabilities may include, but are not limited to:
• Dashboards
• Ad-Hoc reports generation
• Data exports in a number of different formats (.csv, .xlsx, .xls, pdf, etc.)
• Custom SQL queries using a GUI interface and custom scripts.
Configuration
Applications should be configurable so that it requires minimal code change and could be maintained by business users
and Internal IT staff (For example: adding and removing lookup values).
Configurable Business Rules
Provide a dynamic business rules engine to support new application due to ongoing changing business logic or rules, and
the ability to modify business logic without major code changes.
Application Business Logic
The application business layer should handle business logic instead of database layer, a layer that is most scalable,
testable and support versioning.
Application Feedback
Provide a module to collect application user feedback; the business users should have the capability to customize
module fields.
Management screens
All lookup data should be database driven and manageable by admin user.

IMPORTANT NOTE: The vendor is expected to follow standard Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) process, use the traditional
Waterfall development methodology and is expected to deliver a fully functional turnkey solution.
Application Source Code - All source code material developed for this project shall become the sole property of the City of New York and
shall be used for City purposes only.
Overview
This project consists of four (4) modules expected to be developed and released in parallel as part of a three (3) year initial build,
followed by two (2) years of support and maintenance, with one (1) optional extension period for up to two years.
Modules A, B, and C are dependent on each other and share a common data module. As part of the proposal, the vendor must provide
an implementation plan that contains details about how they will resource the project, considering these constraints.
Application Modules
• Module A – Guardian Portal
• Module B – School Portal
• Module C – Admin Portal
• Module D – Child Care Eligibility Portal

18. Vulnerability Management
The Third Party must identify ongoing potential vulnerabilities and security weaknesses in their IT
systems and infrastructure by adopting industry best practices, security controls and procedures. The
Third Party must ensure a repeatable process to address the Vulnerability Management control section.
This may include supporting policies, standards, and procedures.
Ref. Requirements
18.1 The Third Party must include as part of their vulnerability management program the receipt of
vulnerability-related security alerts and intelligence from external and internal sources to identify
and monitor for potential vulnerabilities in their environment.
18.2 Vulnerability scans (authenticated and unauthenticated) must be performed against internal and
external networks and applications periodically and prior to system provisioning for all systems
that process, store, or transmit NYCPS Confidential Information.
18.3 Any critical and high vulnerabilities identified through intelligence gathering, vulnerability scans, or
penetration testing must be prioritized and remediated within a well-defined timeframe
commensurate with the vulnerability risk.
18.4 Third Party should undergo an annual, independent review to assess the practical security of the
Third Party's systems. These reviews should include penetration tests from the perspective of an
external attacker and an internal user with common privileges.
V1.8 [Public] NYC Public Schools Third Party Information Security Requirements page 21

19. Cloud Security
The Third Parties must have strong Identity and Access Management, access control, and authorization
capabilities, as well as compliance, monitoring, and logging capabilities to ensure the ongoing security of
their cloud infrastructure and data. The Third Party must ensure a repeatable process to address the
Cloud Security control section. This may include supporting policies, standards, and procedures.
Requirements
19.1 Root/administrator access to the management console must require multi-factor authentication.
19.2 The Third Party must have the ability to provide logs to NYCPS that are specific to the instances used
for NYCPS or NYCPS engagement.
19.3 Third Party should have all logs in the cloud environment feed into a central log aggregation point
(e.g., Syslog server).
19.4 Third Party should regularly back up application configurations, data within the application, database,
and configuration of systems within cloud infrastructure to ensure that data can be restored if
needed.
19.5 Third Party should support federated authentication (e.g., SAML) or a standards-based identity
protocol (e.g., OpenID Connect, OAuth2, etc.) leveraged for propagating and enforcing identity
controls through the SaaS and API.
19.6 The Third Party must have cryptographic controls implemented to make sure that NYCPS data at rest
within cloud infrastructure is always encrypted (e.g., AES-256).
19.7 Third Party should have mechanisms in place to control encryption key generation, distribution,
storage, access, and destruction.
19.8 The Third Party must take reasonable precautions with a patch management process for (cloud
infrastructure hosting or storing or processing or transmitting NYCPS data) identifying and applying all
relevant security patches and security updates within 30 days of release by the OEM vendor.
V1.8 [Public] NYC Public Schools Third Party Information Security Requirements page 22

Appendix 4 - Technical Standards
Technical Environment
Following is our current technology environment to which the project will be aligned to. Decisions on future
items are to be confirmed by the DOE teams. Additional requirements for application development which
must be adhered to are described in Appendices.
Technology Stack
• Languages: .NET 5+, ASP.NET, HTML5, React/Angular, Azure DevOps
• DOE Integration services: IBM Security Access Manager (ISAM – OpenID/Oauth2.0), DOE RBAC, IBM API Connect,
IBM Integration Bus – IIB, SFTP, REST API
• Reports & Visualization: Power BI
• Databases: SQL Server, Redis
• Geographical Information Systems (GIS): ESRI ArcGIS 10.6.1
• Hosting Environments: OnPrem, Cloud (Preferred provider- Azure)
• Mail Services: SMTP, Email Marketing Software (preferred provider – SendGrid)
• Collaboration & Document Management: Sharepoint/DOE ECM, Microsoft Teams, Confluence, Jira
Integration Between Vendor Solution and DOE Systems
The determination of data integration needs between any vendor proposed solution and DOE Systems will be done in
collaboration with the DOE DIIT teams to ensure standard best practices and modern data integration technologies such
as interoperable Service Bus, REST API integration, SFTP, rules engine capabilities and best in class ETL toolsets for
upstream and downstream data integration. The actual number of the data feeds and data elements shall be identified
and confirmed during the discovery phase of this project.
Additionally, the vendor will be responsible for all applicable data exchange between existing DOE services and the
vendor solution to the DOE databases in support operations of upstream and downstream systems.
WCAG Compliance
New application must be compliant with Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG 2.0 AA), so it can be accessible by
users with disabilities.
• Application may require multi-lingual support (10 languages)
• Design within DOE style Guide
Responsive Design
Application must be responsive from desktops, mobile devices like tablets and cell phones and compatible with all
modern browsers regardless of the settings of the browsers. Below is the list of mobile devices and supported browsers.
Supported Mobile Devices:
Mobile Devices* (Latest/Latest-1)
• Apple Devices:
o iPhone 15 Pro / Pro Max
o iPhone 15 / 15 Plus

SECTION. 1 PROGRAM SUMMARY
INTRODUCTION
The New York City (“NYC”) Department of Education (“DOE) is the largest school system in the country, serving over 1.1 million children
across 1,800 schools. The mission of the DOE is to work collaboratively with parents, educators, school communities, and external
stakeholders to improve student achievement and ensure that every child graduates from high school prepared for college, a career,
and a future as a productive, critically thinking adult.
The vision of the DOE is to provide Equity and Excellence for all of our students and the DOE has launched a range of initiatives to further
this vision, including but not limited to Pre-K for All, the Community Schools Program, as well as other Equity and Excellence Initiatives.
The objective of this solicitation is to contract for professional services through GSA Multiple Award Schedule: Special Item Number:
54151S Information Technology Professional Services.
The MySchools Modernization Project will rebuild the MySchools application. As the cornerstone of New York City’s school admissions
processes, the current system no longer meets the DOE’s needs and poses significant challenges, including outdated technology and
limited scalability. These issues hinder the ability to deliver seamless user experiences, adapt to evolving policy needs, and meet the
increasing demands of users during peak periods.
This project will deliver a modernized platform that aligns with current technology standards, ensuring long-term sustainability,
improved system performance, student information security and reduced operational overhead. The new system will enhance usability
for families, streamline workflows for schools, and provide robust tools for administrative staff to manage admissions processes with
greater efficiency. Rebuilding the system will not only address the technical and operational challenges of the current system but also
position the DOE to deliver a seamless, efficient, and equitable admissions experience for all stakeholders, and further the mission of
providing quality education to every student.
The Contractor is expected to provide a complete turnkey solution, including but not limited to software, design, implementation, and
professional services to create the new system. The vendor is free to utilize custom-developed components, Commercial Off-the-Shelf
solutions (COTS), or a combination of both, as long as the proposed solution meets all the business requirements listed in this document.
The awarded contract(s) will be for a term of three (3) years. The DOE reserves the option to renew the contract for one (1) additional
two-year period. The DOE anticipates awarding a single vendor for a maximum term of seven (5) years.
SECTION. 2 MINIMUM QUALIFICATIONS
All proposals received on or before the due date and time will be evaluated to determine whether they meet the following Minimum
Qualifications. Proposals that fail to meet all minimum qualifications will not be considered and will be found non-responsive.
• Proposer(s) must hold a General Services Administration (“GSA”) contracts under GSA Multiple Award Schedule: Special Item
Number: 54151S Information Technology Professional Services
• Proposer(s) must be a for-profit or not-for-profit institution. Individuals are not eligible to submit proposals for this solicitation.
• Proposer(s) must have a minimum of three (3) years of experience in providing such consulting-based services.
• Proposals with any exceptions to the terms and conditions mentioned herein will be disqualified and therefore will not be
evaluated if the exceptions are deemed material to the scope of services. The Cover Letter of the proposal should clearly specify
that the Proposer is not raising any exceptions to the terms and conditions mentioned in this solicitation.
IF THE SUBMITTED PROPOSAL DOES NOT CLEARLY EXHIBIT ALL OF THE ABOVE, THEN THE PROPOSAL WILL NOT BE FURTHER EVALUATED.
SECTION. 3 SCOPE OF SERVICES
3.1 PURPOSE & OBJECTIVES
The overall purpose of this project is to modernize the MySchools platform and its associated portals to better support DOE’s admissions
and enrollment processes. This initiative focuses on improving operational efficiency, aligning systems with evolving policy
requirements, and providing a seamless experience for families, schools, and administrators.

Benefits to Agency
• Transforming and improving the user experience: Create a streamlined, intuitive interface across all portals, ensuring accessibility,
simplicity, and responsiveness for families, schools, and administrators.
• Automated workflows and self-service functionality: Enable role-based access, automated notifications, and efficient workflows
to minimize manual intervention and enhance user independence.
• Data integration and consistency: Strengthen synchronization with DOE enterprise systems, ensuring accurate and up-to-date
data across all platforms.
• Compliance with regulatory mandates: Implement features to meet federal, state, and local requirements, including automated
reporting and policy-driven updates.
• Scalability and performance: Refactor the platform with a modern, scalable architecture to handle peak usage periods and
support future enhancements.
High-level project goals:
• Increased System Reliability and Scalability: The modernized system will be designed to handle peak usage periods with improved
performance and minimized downtime, providing uninterrupted access to critical services.
• Expanded Business Functions: New capabilities, such as enhanced authentication, role-based access control, and improved portal
functionalities, will address evolving policy needs and empower administrators and families.
• Operational Efficiency: Consolidated workflows, automated processes, and reduced technical debt will allow DOE to focus
resources on delivering value-driven enhancements instead of maintaining an outdated platform.
• Improved Data Accuracy: Enhancements will improve data consistency and integration with other DOE enterprise systems,
ensuring accurate, real-time information for operational and reporting needs.
• Advanced Reporting and Analytics: Develop robust reporting functionalities to support data-driven decision-making and
compliance with federal, state, and city mandates.
• Robust Security Controls: Implement and maintain enterprise-level security protocols to protect the system from cyber threats
and ensure data confidentiality.
• Future-Proofing: Built on a flexible and scalable technology stack, the system will adapt to policy changes, future innovations, and
the evolving needs of DOE.
• Seamless Integration with enterprise capabilities: Integrate with DOE’s enterprise Authentication and authorization capabilities.
DIIT will spearhead this initiative and manage the day-to-day activities for this project. DIIT will work with the vendor to ensure all in-
scope requirements are successfully achieved. In addition, deliverables will include plans for end user training, adoption and retention.

7. Data Protection and Retention
The Third Party must ensure the protection of data confidentiality, integrity, and availability across all
applications, transactions, and reports by implementing security measures for data in transit, in process,
and at rest by adhering to best practices, procedures and standards. The Third Party must ensure a
repeatable process to address the Confidential Information Authorization control section. This may
include supporting policies, standards, and procedures.
Ref. Requirements
7.1 Third Parties and its subcontractors should have controls in place to allow NYCPS to validate that
a complete set of data has been received as agreed-upon in the data-processing agreement.
7.2 All NYCPS data provided to and stored by the Third Party and dependent subcontractors must be
stored and retained in a manner that:
- includes the capability to access and retrieve the data as needed.
- avoids loss due to media decay or technology obsolescence.
- provides reasonable safeguards against ordinary hazards, and man-made hazards/disasters.
- is in accordance with applicable laws, regulations, and contractual obligations.
- protects the data from unauthorized access/alteration.
7.3 Third Parties may be required to support DOE’s obligation to retain Confidential Information
pursuant to LGS-1, available at http://www.archives.nysed.gov/records/local-government-
record-schedule/lgs-1-title-page.
7.4 The Third Party is required to retain, transfer, and destroy Confidential Information as agreed
upon in the written agreement with NYCPS.

BACKGROUND
The Division of Instructional & Information Technology (“DIIT”) plays a crucial role in helping the DOE execute its mission and vision by
providing technology that enables equity and excellence. DIIT maintains a collection of applications to support our students and families.
Each year, over 460,000 unique students from New York City’s public, private, and parochial schools apply for admission to more than
10,000 public school programs for the upcoming school year and summer term. This process involves a common framework with custom
requirements for different programs. Parents and guardians browse a directory of eligible programs, rank their preferences, and submit
applications on behalf of the students. The Gale-Shapley Deferred Acceptance algorithm assigns students to programs, with adjustments
made to optimize outcomes. After assignments are completed, families can accept or decline placements and add their child to waitlists
for other programs. Schools manage these waitlists and extend offers as seats become available.
Since 2018, these processes have been managed through a web-based application that offers an intuitive, mobile-friendly experience,
supports ten languages, and allows DOE program offices to quickly adapt workflows and business rules. While the current system has
been instrumental in streamlining the admissions process and providing valuable functionalities, it now requires modernization to
continue meeting the growing needs of users. Built on a specialized technology stack, the system faces challenges in aligning with DOE’s
standard technology, which has increased maintenance complexity and costs. Additionally, peak usage periods have proven the need
for a new, performance-focused system design to ensure a seamless experience for families.
The existing application, in place for nearly six years, has served its purpose well but now stands ready for an upgrade to enhance the
user experience. To keep pace with technological advancements and the increasing demands of users, a transition to a modern, DOE -
aligned technology stack is necessary. Enhancing the system's ability to handle heavy user loads during critical periods will further
improve its reliability and performance, ensuring consistent service during major launch and deadline days. This will directly impact end
users by providing faster processing times, reducing system downtime, and offering a more intuitive and accessible user interface.
The selected vendor will deliver a modernized MySchools system. This includes providing any necessary licenses, software, architecture,
and design, as well as deploying the system within DOE’s environment. The vendor will collaborate closely with DOE to implement the
system, manage the project, conduct analysis, design the architecture, ensure best practices are followed, and train DOE users on
maintaining and utilizing the new system. This modernization effort aims to build on the strengths of the current application while
addressing its limitations, ultimately making the admissions process smoother and more efficient for families navigating through it.
3.2 EXSISTING SYSTEM DESCRIPTION
The MySchools platform is a comprehensive online system that supports the admissions processes for over 460,000 unique students
across New York City Public School’s programs each year. It provides families, schools, and administrators with tools to manage
applications, placements, waitlists, and program-specific requirements. This section outlines the platform’s current functionality, key
components, and the workflows it supports.
Supported Admissions Process
The MySchools supports a wide range of admissions processes tailored to meet the diverse needs of students and families across New
York City. These processes span various grade levels, specialized programs, and unique admissions workflows, accommodating the
City's vast and dynamic public school population. The table below provides an overview of each process, including a brief description
and the approximate number of applicants served annually.
NAME DESCRIPTION # APPLICANTS*

admission to Summer Rising programs to take place during the upcoming summer.

6 OSE/School This function is required for schools to verify claimed status. The enrollment guidelines, in Chancellor’s
Eligibility regulation A-101, prioritize keeping siblings together, and families have the right to claim siblings (including
Review those with different names). Schools who know the students directly can review that claim, and the central
office additionally reviews home address, current school, and parent/guardian data in ATS to ensure that
families can stay together in the Pre-K to 5th grades.
7 Gale-Shapley Since 2003, the DOE has had considerable success applying a matching process based on a class of
Matching procedures called Gale-Shapley Deferred Acceptance Algorithms (“the Algorithm”). This process is
Algorithm customized to the assignment of students to programs and is based on a wide variety of criteria. There are
rules (criteria) that define who is eligible for a program, and then limits the number of assignments to each
program and assigns students in order of that predetermined criteria
8 Review of Manual input from DOE staff is sometimes necessary to assign students upon completion of the Algorithm
Outcomes and a new placement is then required. For example, data entry issues do not invalidate Algorithmic
execution. In these cases, DOE staff would need to manually apply the algorithm, and reassign a student
based on those results. Furthermore, quality assurance reviews need to be performed to ensure that letters
populate placement results correctly, and that translated documentation is accurate.
9 Results Upon confirmation of the completion of a round of admissions, letters are mailed home and made available
Released on the parent-facing website for real-time access. DOE publishes overall results publicly and performs
analyses on outcome trends.
10 Student These are submitted to the system after consideration of the results. For elementary processes, this
Responses requires pre-registration at the school directly. For Middle and High school enrollment, students are
assigned to ATS and other DOE databases in preparation for the next school year.
Gale-Shapley Matching Algorithm
The MySchools matching algorithm is based on the deferred acceptance algorithm. This algorithm prioritizes applicants based on
predefined admission priorities and ensures fairness by considering both program capacity and applicant preferences. It is designed to
allocate seats in programs by processing applicants in rounds based on their priority group and randomly assigned tie-breaking
numbers. If an applicant cannot secure a seat in a higher-priority program, they are deferred to their next program choice while still
being considered for future rounds. This process ensures that each applicant is given the best possible placement according to their
preferences, priorities, and the availability of seats.

System Integrations
High-level integrations with internal DOE systems and description of data to be exchanged (but not limited to):
• Automate the Schools (ATS) / NextGen Student Information System (NGSIS) - Primary student information system for PK–
12 DOE public/ charter schools and programs. DOE is currently transitioning from the legacy system, ATS, to a modern solution,
NGSIS. Data retrieved from ATS/NGSIS is used to validate student records in MySchools, as well as supply students’ biographical
and current school enrollment data used in enrollment processes. Data sent from MySchools to ATS/NGSIS includes students
who are new to DOE systems and require a student identification record in ATS, as well as those who have received and
accepted offers to schools in MySchools and now require official registration of enrollment to the schools in ATS/NGSIS.
• Special Education Student Information System (SESIS) - Individualized Education Program (IEP) information management
system for students ages 5+. Data retrieved from SESIS includes information about students requiring specialized support used
for eligibility and matching.
• Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) - DOE's system for managing staff location and role assignment for the purpose of application
authorization and permissions. Data retrieved from RBAC includes information about DOE employees who are authorized to
perform administrative actions on behalf of specific schools in MySchools.
• New York City Schools Account (NYCSA) - NYCSA is a family-facing online application that provides authorized and authenticated
families access to key information about their students’ school records and can be viewed in any of ten DOE language
translations. NYCSA displays up-to-date information on attendance records, report card grades, assessments, emergency
contact information and more to help track student progress throughout the school year. Data retrieved from NYCSA will
include information about adults who are authorized to perform enrollment actions in MySchools on behalf of specific
students. NYCSA will serve as a unified platform for families to apply for enrollment. As part of the integration, MySchools will
leverage NYCSA for account creation and authentication through a unified login process, reducing the complexity of managing
multiple credentials and ensuring families experience a streamlined platform for enrollment-related processes.
• Location Code Generation Management System (LCGMS) - DOE’s official record of school locations. Data retrieved from LCGMS
is used to populate and display school attributes in MySchools such as name, address, district, and grades served.
• Enrollment Apps (Register Projections System & Seat Targets) - MySchools and the Enrollment Apps system are not currently
integrated but should exchange critical data in the future state to improve alignment and streamline enrollment planning. The
Register Projections System (RPS), which forecasts student enrollment numbers, and Seat Targets, which defines seat capacities
for school programs, would receive data on school programs from MySchools to ensure accurate projections and allocations.
In turn, MySchools would receive seat target data from Seat Targets to reflect capacity during the admissions process.
• GeoSupport Service – DOE’s services for returning geo-related data for a given address. The GeoSupport service is called when
school, guardian, or student addresses change in MySchools and returns data used in admissions such as latitude, longitude,
district, borough, and zoned schools for the given address.
• MyCity Childcare API – MyCity is a City of New York portal for accessing City services and benefits, including subsidized child
care programs. MySchools integrates with MyCity to retrieve childcare applications and supporting documents submitted
through the MyCity portal. These materials are made available in MySchools to case managers within the DOE's Division of
Early Childhood Education for review and eligibility determination.
• Early Childhood Management System (ECMS) – A new system is being developed to manage contracts and services for
contracted early childhood providers. MySchools will transmit data to the Early Childhood Management System (ECMS) on
students who have accepted offers to participate in relevant early childhood programs.
• DOE’s Enterprise Licensed Mapping and Emailing Services – DOE maintains licensing for enterprise mapping and emailing
services. MySchools will leverage these existing services to implement relevant functionality, such as serving interactive maps
and sending transactional emails to users.
• Service Cloud – OSE currently uses Service Cloud to track visits to Family Welcome Centers and calls to the P311 call center
related to admissions and enrollment, but MySchools and Service Cloud are not currently integrated. In the future state,
integration may include sharing data to display records of Family Welcome Center visits or P311 calls within MySchools and
showing the status of MySchools applications in Service Cloud. This integration would enhance data accessibility and streamline
support processes across both systems.

Hardcoded Text The current system relies on hardcoded text for many areas, making even simple updates cumbersome and
Dependencies dependent on development resources. This inflexibility delays updates, adds costs, and reduces the system’s
ability to adapt quickly to policy changes or user needs. The new system will introduce configurable text
management, reducing dependency on development resources and enabling faster updates.
Inefficient With the current system, managing translations across ten languages is a highly manual process that cannot
Translation keep up with frequent text changes. This can lead to delays, inconsistencies, and errors, creating challenges
Management for equitable access by non-English-speaking families. The project will implement centralized tools to
streamline updates and ensure consistency across languages.
Manual Waitlist Schools are required to manually extend offers to students on waitlists when seats become available,
Management creating inefficiencies and increasing the likelihood of errors or delays. This process can overwhelm school

management, enabling timely and accurate offers without manual intervention.
Rigid School Year The system architecture does not support overlapping school years, requiring prior-year processes (e.g.,
Architecture waitlists) to be closed before configuration for the next year can begin. This results in a compressed timeline
for configuration, testing, and launch, limiting operational flexibility and preventing the DOE from responding
to families’ requests to move admissions timelines earlier. The modernized system will support overlapping
school year configurations, providing more flexibility for planning and testing.
Misaligned The current platform is built on a specialized, outdated technology stack that is difficult to maintain and does
Technology Stack not align with DOE standards. This mismatch complicates routine maintenance, integrations, and the
system’s ability to adopt new features or performance optimizations. The project will align the technology
stack with DOE standards, improving maintainability and enabling seamless integration with modern tools.
Peak Usage Strain During critical periods such as application deadlines and result releases, the system struggles to handle high
traffic. This leads to slow performance or downtime, reducing user trust and creating additional workload for
support staff. The redesigned system will incorporate scalability enhancements to handle peak traffic without
performance issues.

3.3 PROJECT SCOPE
The MySchools Modernization Project aims to transition the existing platform to a more robust, modern, scalable, and user-focused
application. This initiative ensures parity with current functionality while addressing critical areas such as reliability, system
compatibility, and evolving policy-driven requirements. The project will deliver a system capable of supporting families, schools,
administrators, and childcare eligibility workflows, while maintaining operational continuity during high-visibility enrollment
milestones.
To achieve this, the DOE seeks a vendor with expertise in large-scale IT system implementations, adhering to fixed timelines, and
aligning with DOE technology stack, protocols, and cybersecurity standards. The vendor will provide the necessary technical resources
to ensure the system is delivered efficiently and with minimal disruption to end users.
The vendor is expected to provide project management and technical support for various activities, including (but not limited to):
• Conducting requirements analysis to document current system functionality and identify opportunities for optimization and
modernization.
• Designing, developing, and implementing the new system using a flexible and scalable architecture aligned with DOE’s
technology stack.
• Creating comprehensive workflows and integrating with enterprise systems to ensure seamless data sharing and operational
efficiency.
• Performing thorough functional, regression, performance, and security testing to validate system reliability and scalability
during high-traffic periods.
• Integrating with DOE systems as required
• Providing maintenance and continuous technical support through the duration of the project.

Figure 7 – Conceptual Future State Architecture (Modular Monolith with Onion Design)
Below is a summary of the high-level components represented in the onion architecture diagram:
Component Description
Domain Model The innermost layer, representing the core business logic and entities of the application.
Domain Services Handles business rules and domain-specific operations, ensuring the separation of business logic.
Repositories Provides a layer to interact with data persistence systems, abstracting database operations.
Application Services Coordinates use cases and interactions between domain logic and external systems or interfaces.
22

Infrastructure Contains implementation details such as database connections, APIs, and messaging systems.
API Services Manages communication between internal modules and external systems using well-defined
endpoints.
Presentation Layer Front-end technologies (e.g., React, Angular) to build user-facing interfaces.
Web Services Includes integrations such as SSO, SMTP, and other web-based services to support application needs.
Data Sources External databases and data providers that store and manage application data.
Libraries Reusable components or tools that provide common functionalities across the application.
Reporting Supports the generation of reports based on application data and user needs.
External Services Integration points with third-party services, such as external APIs or enterprise services.
Tests Includes unit, integration, and system tests to ensure application quality and stability.
Enterprise Service Bus Facilitates communication and data exchange between the system and external services.
(ESB)

The information presented below is the current draft of functional and business requirements needed for the new system/application.
Module A – Guardian Portal – High-Level Scope
The Guardian Portal is a family-facing platform that empowers parents and guardians to manage their students' school enrollment
and admissions. It offers tools for exploring school and program options, submitting and tracking applications, responding to offers,
and managing waitlists. The portal provides multilingual support and ensures families have access to all relevant information and
updates through a user-friendly, accessible interface.

Figure 6 – MySchools Infrastructure Overview
CATEGORY TECHNOLOGY
AVI LOAD BALANCER Distributes incoming traffic across multiple servers to ensure high availability and
reliability.
WEB SERVERS Hosts application logic and interfaces for guardians, schools, and administrators.
Includes:
• Web-Guardians: Servers dedicated to handling guardian-facing portal requests.
• Web-Schools: Servers for school-facing portal interactions.
• Web-Admin: Servers supporting administrative functionalities.
MEDIA SERVER Stores and serves media files required for application functionality.
REDIS SERVER Provides caching to enhance application performance and reduce database load.
POSTGRES DATABASE The primary database for storing structured application data.
WORKERS Handles asynchronous tasks such as data processing, email notifications, and
scheduled jobs.
SERV-U FTP Facilitates secure file transfers and integration with external systems.
IBM FTP Manages file transfers with legacy systems and third-party services.
SAN (STORAGE AREA NETWORK) Centralized storage system for backups, media, and database files.
SECURITY COMPONENTS Includes multiple layers of security:
• Antivirus Management: Protects infrastructure against malicious software
• Malware Management: Identifies and mitigates threats from malware.
• Intrusion Prevention: Monitors and prevents unauthorized access attempts.
• Penetration Testing: Regular testing to identify and address vulnerabilities.
• Vulnerability Assessment: Continuous assessment of infrastructure security.
MONITORING & ALERT MANAGEMENT Tracks system performance and raises alerts for issues requiring immediate attention.
DEPLOYMENT & RELEASE MANAGEMENT Manages application deployments and ensures consistent version control.

Delivering detailed technical and user documentation, along with training and knowledge transfer for smooth operational handoff.
The solution must have the following characteristics:
• Accessibility across mobile devices and major browsers
• Scalability to accommodate peak usage
• Extensibility of the technology architecture (e.g., use of open standards) to support integration with DOE systems.
• Interoperability with DOE systems for management and reporting processes

The services will be performed under hourly rate contracts within the GSA contract guidelines.
All project resources must be based within the continental United States. Work can be conducted either onsite or virtually, as

When on-site presence of the Contractor is required, will be provided at 335 Adams Street, Brooklyn NY 11201, 2 Metrotech Center,
Brooklyn NY 11201 or alternate DIIT locations within the five boroughs of the New York City area, as determined by DOE.
Travel expenses will not be reimbursed by the DOE.
Employers are required by Federal law to verify that all employees are legally entitled to work in the United States of America.
Accordingly, the Issuing Entity reserves the right to request, from the legally mandated employer, documentation attesting to the
same for each consultant that’s assigned work under any project awarded as a result of this solicitation. In accord with such laws, the
Issuing Entity does not discriminate against individuals on the basis of national origin or citizenship.
CONCEPTUAL ARCHITECTURE
For the MySchools Modernization Project, the DOE prefers modular monolith architecture, which provides a single deployable unit
with clearly defined and encapsulated modules. This approach simplifies deployment while maintaining strong separation of concerns,
making it easier to manage, scale, and evolve over time. The modular monolith supports robust maintainability, minimizes
interdependencies, and enables efficient testing and debugging without the complexities associated with distributed systems.
Proposals must include a detailed technical architecture approach that best meets the objectives and requirements of the solicitation.

Business Processes and System Database Interactions
The following diagram depicts the end-to-end enrollment process and associated activities for both school-aged and early childhood
students.
Figure 3 - Centralized Enrollment Process (School-aged Students)
STEP NAME HIGH-LEVEL DESCRIPTION
1 DOE Student Includes gathering of all existing student information/data that DOE databases currently house. Creating
Information these connections enables DOE to inform families of eligibility, disclose student information which impacts
Databases enrollment, and for DOE to ensure the applicant pool is as representative of as many eligible students as
possible
2 Register for A pre-application registration for tests and auditions which are required to determine student eligibility is a
Testing functionality of MySchools. The Specialized High School Admission Test (SHSAT) is the current test that OSE
manages registration for, which also involves reviewing eligibility, test scheduling and assignment logistics
and ensuring proper testing accommodations are provided for all students. This feature would be expanded
to include any school which has an arts or music auditions or portfolio submissions, as well as schools who
administer any type of student assessment.
3 Application Involves the ways students and family interface with DOE to submit application choices. This includes calling
Access the P311 call center, visiting a Family Welcome Center, or directly accessing the parent-facing website.
4 Application This is the integration of separate OSE innovations directly into the application submission experience.
Submission These tools steer students and families to submitting the best possible application without the consultation
Management of other resources outside of the application itself.
a. Travel Time Tools – This will take information of home address and inform students of travel time
requirements of an application choice.
b. Eligibility Guidance and Warnings – Similar to travel time indicators, application best practices can
be shared with families to further support the most informed applications. For example, students
who choose only 1-2 schools, or only the most selective schools, are more at risk of being
unmatched.
c. Translations in 9 languages – DOE currently translates all communications in Arabic, Bengali,
Chinese, French, Haitian-Creole, Korean, Russian, Spanish, and Urdu.
d. Schools’ directory – Currently, DOE publishes physical school directories for all enrollment
processes and has been piloting solutions to the limitations of reach and access. The integration of
a school directory finding aid directly into the application process allows searched programs to be
directly added to an application, in tandem with the other resources. This is a feature that is
available to all admissions directories and reduces the investment stress for parents that do not
have the time or savvy to navigate the processes and improve outcomes by a “one stop shopping”
experience.
5 Submitted OSE receives applications, and reviews for accuracy and accounts for missing students/applications who
Application would be adversely affected by non-participation. The application review is a central office function.

3.5 PROJECT DELIVERABLES
The selected vendor will be responsible for activities necessary to deliver the full set of business deliverables on time and in
accordance with industry best practices and those unique to the DOE.
The vendor is expected to follow the standard Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) process and use the Waterfall development
methodology and is expected to deliver a fully functional turnkey solution.
• The Waterfall methodology that will be utilized for the project is a traditional waterfall approach; it will not be an adapted
waterfall, an agile/waterfall hybrid, etc.
• There will be no iterative development. The design, development, and deployment will all be completed in a structured
sequential manner. Work will not be completed in sprints. All project development phases such as design, development, testing,
etc. are completed once.
• There will be no changes made to the project development requirements after the initial planning has been completed. Testing
will come after the build phase and will not be performed concurrently with programming.
• Once a module is put into the production environment, no additional changes will be made.
• Application Source Code - All source code material developed for this project shall become the sole property of the City of New
York and shall be used for City purposes only.
The vendor will also be responsible for the following:
• Complete testing of the system except for User Acceptance Testing (UAT) which will be a DOE and VENDOR combined effort.
• Project Repository, an archive creation and delivery of all project artifacts.
• End user training documentation (for both front end customers and backend technical support) including but not limited to
training manuals, walk through videos/demos etc.
This project consists of four (4) modules expected to be developed and released in parallel as part of a three (3) year initial build,
followed by a two (2) year optional renewal for support and maintenance.
Modules A, B, and C are dependent on each other and share a common data module. As part of the proposal, the vendor must
provide an implementation plan that contains details about how they will resource the project, considering these constraints.
See Appendix 6 – Project Deliverables
All deliverables must be in an industry standard, professional, editable format that may be reasonably maintained by DOE. This format
may differ depending on the deliverable. For each deliverable, the Vendor should specify the format they intend to deliver it in, and
the tools they will use to develop it.
The process for DOE accepting all deliverables (those described in the appendix and any other deliverables the Vendor identifies in
their response) should be described in response to this project.
Payment will be made to the Contractor after a written verification of completion of a deliverable is made by DOE’s Project Manager.
• Deliverables will be due on dates mutually agreed to set in the project plan.
• DOE will review and respond to notification from the Contractor of completed deliverables within five (5) business days of
receiving them.
• The Contractor will have three (3) business days to schedule/address needed revisions.
• DOE will review the final version within two (2) business days.
Every attempt will be made to be clear about expectations and to avoid redoing deliverables multiple times.
3.6 RESOURCE REQUIREMENTS
The DOE expects the awarded vendor to identify the essential staff roles needed to achieve the project goals and to coordinate
effectively with the DOE project team. Vendors should propose resources that align with their tailored solutions to meet the DOE's
requirements.
Specifically, the NYC DOE anticipates that the System Integrator’s project approach and pricing will reflect strong collaboration with
the DOE to develop the necessary infrastructure and thoroughly analyze business deliverables.

3.4 PROJECT MANAGEMENT REQUIREMENTS
The vendor will be responsible for meeting all project deliverables and milestones, with payment contingent upon the DOE’s written
acceptance of these outcomes.
The vendor will lead the preparation of a comprehensive project plan outlining specific milestones and deliverables, which must be
submitted for DOE approval.
Beyond written deliverables, the vendor will conduct a formal debriefing of assessment results for an audience designated by the
DOE, as outlined in the final agreement. Communication may occur in person, via phone, virtually, or through email, with the
frequency and content tailored to meet the specific needs of each engagement.
The selected vendor is expected to provide robust project management and quality control measures, including:
• Reporting project status and escalating issues as necessary
• Managing all vendor resources
• Addressing risks, scope changes, issues, and project modifications
• Troubleshooting and problem-solving across all project aspects
• Coordinating efforts between the vendor and the DOE
• Reviewing all deliverables and work products for quality and compliance with the final Scope of Work (SOW)
• Documenting meeting minutes and project discussions
The DOE expects the vendor to designate a Project Manager who will serve as the single point of contact for the vendor. This
individual will report to the DOE Project Manager and be responsible for:
• Overseeing work assignments, schedules, budgets, and team performance throughout the project, ensuring the quality of services
delivered
• Ensuring the quality and acceptance of all project deliverables by the DOE Project Manager
• Reporting all significant project developments, with the reporting format and distribution methods agreed upon at the project’s
outset
• Creating work plans that outline tasks, responsibilities, and risk mitigation strategies
• Maintaining staff continuity throughout the project, with any replacements requiring DOE Project Manager approval
• Overseeing knowledge transfer activities to ensure DOE’s comprehensive understanding of all project aspects
• Handling its own administrative support, as DOE will not provide these services or materials
• Supplying any necessary tools or equipment for vendor staff
• Storing all project-related documentation in the DOE-designated repository
The vendor will provide weekly status reports and monthly steering committee status reports. Weekly status reports will include
accomplishments for the reporting period, upcoming activities to be accomplished during the next reporting period, a major issue and
risk summary, a project change request summary, a milestone status report, and an updated project schedule.
The Project Managers are responsible for coordinating with Vendor project teams and working with DOE teams to produce a
comprehensive status report.

FINGERPRINTING
State education laws and DOE policy and practice mandate the taking of fingerprints as a prerequisite for licensure and/or
employment. Particular attention is given to fingerprint results that reveal criminal history. Currently, this is the process
http://schools.nyc.gov/Offices/DHR/Fingerprinting+Employee+IDs+and+Smart+Cards.htm.
Applicants must be fingerprinted to become a consultant or obtain employment with the New York City Department of Education
(DOE), whether they are hired as individuals or as part of a project. Cleared fingerprints do not guarantee employment with DOE or
with a vendor providing services to the DOE. Cleared fingerprint results and eligibility for employment may change depending on the
outcome of any investigations, as well as any arrests or changes in your DOE employment status that may have occurred after the
date you were printed.
The HR Connect Walk-In Center and the Office of Personnel Investigation (OPI) are responsible for screening all new staff hired by the
New York City Department of Education (DOE) to ensure the safety and well-being of students and staff. Consultants cannot go to the
HR Connect Walk-in Center for fingerprinting unless they have completed all of their required forms online. See section on “PETS”
below for further details. No walk-ins will be accepted for fingerprinting.
To be fingerprinted, the Applicant must have:
a. Proper identification - All applicants must provide a valid government-issued photo ID to be fingerprinted.
b. Payment for processing fee - The cost for fingerprinting at the HR-Connect Walk-In Center is $115 (subject to change) and must
be covered by the Contractor. The DOE is not responsible for Fingerprinting fees. Fingerprinting fees are also non-refundable.
Acceptable forms of payment are:
c. Money orders (payable to DOE/DHRT).
d. Personal checks (payable to DOE/DHRT).
e. Credit card (except American Express) or debit card with a logo (some exceptions may apply).
PETS – PERSONNEL ELIGIBILITY TRACKING SYSTEM
This is the DOE’s web-based database that allows the Contractor or DOE Staff to enter Contractor Staff information into a roster (the
“PETS Roster”) in order to monitor the fingerprinting status of each Contractor Staff member.
All Contractor “Staff” must undergo fingerprinting and a background check by the DOE Office of Personnel Investigation Unit. Staff
shall include any and all of Vendor’s, or its subcontractors’ (if any), employees, officers, directors, members, partners, agents, or
consultants who, at any time during the Term of the agreement, have direct contact with Students; or work in or visit a location during
times that Students are present unless such contact is only on an incidental and supervised basis; or provide online services to
Students and have contact with Students via telephone, email or internet; or have regular access to Students’ confidential information
and data. Vendors shall also include any Staff who becomes affiliated with Vendor or its Subcontractors after execution of the
Agreement and who applies to the aforementioned criteria.
Contractors or the DOE have an obligation to enter Contractor staff information into the PETS roster before staff starts providing
services and staff security clearance must be verified as a condition of employment/acceptance of award in Tier II and update PETS
roster as new staff members are hired and as staff status changes.

CONFIDENTIALITY
Prior to onboarding, each project resource will be required to sign a non-disclosure agreement.
3.7 DELIVERABLE ACCEPTANCE REQUIREMENTS
Contractor will provide written verification of completion of a deliverable is made by DOE’s Project Manager.
• Deliverables will be due on dates mutually agreed to set in the project plan.
• DOE will review and respond to notification from the Contractor of completed deliverables within five (5) business days of
receiving them.
• The Contractor will have three (3) business days to schedule/address needed revisions.
• DOE will review the final version within two (2) business days.
Every attempt will be made to be clear about expectations and to avoid redoing deliverables multiple times.
3.8 INVOICING REQUIREMENTS
Contractor shall invoice DOE for each deliverable upon its acceptance. Invoices must be submitted to DIIT Accounts Payable Unit with
a copy to the Project Manager upon completion of each deliverable.
Invoices must contain the contract number, Project Title, work performed, performance period, amount due, total billed to date.
DOE will require confirmation from the Project Manager that the DOE has accepted the deliverable prior to payment of the invoice for
that deliverable.
Invoices will be emailed to DIITinvoices@schools.nyc.gov.
All invoices will be submitted on company letterhead and contain the following information:
1. The Contractor’s name, address and contact name and phone number must be prominently displayed — NOTE: The Contractor’s
name on the invoice must be on file with the NYC Comptroller’s Office via a W-9 form submission. If a W-9 is not on file, please
contact the Requesting Agency for further instructions.
2. Contractor: Contract Pin number and the FMS number must be on the invoices.
3. Invoice number and date of invoice
4. Invoice service period and date of delivery
5. Invoices must be submitted in both hard copy and soft copy format.
6. Detailed description of service must be in agreement with the contract.
7. Appropriate backup and all other supporting documentation to substantiate the invoice charges must be attached:
• Payment Milestones: For work provided on a fixed-price basis, with milestone payments, documentation evidencing the
City’s acceptance of the applicable payment milestone(s) must be included with the invoice.
• Report of the number of hours, roles and resource(s) used to complete each deliverable.

NAME HIGH-LEVEL DESCRIPTION
Self Service Families learn about the application process(es) relevant to their student(s) and identify schools and programs of
Discovery interest by attending events, engaging with Office of Student Enrollment-managed communications, and
interacting with the MySchools school search functionality.
Admission Families use the MySchools application to apply their student(s) to schools and programs of interest. The Office of
Student Enrollment matches applicants to available program seats using the Gale-Shapley Matching Algorithm and
generates offers to students. Families accept their offers or use waitlists to be considered for programs pending
seat availability and selection.
Enrollment Students admitted to programs via MySchools are officially enrolled in the school and program via Automate the
Schools (ATS), a separate DOE system.
Transfer Students may transfer to other schools for a variety of reasons. These enrollment changes are recorded in ATS.
Process Throughout the year, families receive enrollment-related support from DOE central office staff and school
Support administrators.
Analysis & All data collected from MySchools and other supporting systems must be readily available to DOE central
Reporting administrators year-round. Data is used to report on enrollment-related activity, compliance, and more.

SECTION. 7 EVALUATION METHODOLOGY
7.1 EVALUATION METHODOLOGY
BASIS OF AWARD
Proposals will be accepted from firms actively engaged in providing the services specified in this solicitation. DOE reserves the right,
before making an award, to take any action necessary to determine a bidder’s fitness, reliability and ability to perform the services
requested by the DOE.
All proposals received by the due date and time and at the location specified in this solicitation will be evaluated to determine whether
the proposing entity meets the Minimum Qualification Requirements. Submissions that fail to show that the Minimum Qualification
Requirements are met will not receive further consideration. Proposers must submit supporting documentation with their responses
which are required by the Minimum Qualification Requirements.
Contract awards will be based upon the “Best Value Method” reviewing quality, cost and efficiency and the timely completion of contract
negotiations between the Agency/Departments and the selected proposer(s).
An award will be made to the Contractor(s) who offers the best value solution. Below are the criteria which will be used to evaluate
responses which meet the Minimum Qualifications Requirements:
• Technical – 70%
• Financial – 30%
DOE reserves the right not to award any contracts.
DOE reserves the right to award multiple vendors.
SHORTLIST
An evaluation committee will read and evaluate proposals and select a short list of Proposers who may be invited to present their
solution and team. The proposers who are invited will be able to present their solution, explain or make clarifications regarding their
response and their staff to the evaluation committee. After the presentation, Proposers will be asked to resubmit a revised proposal
and budget as their Best and Final Offer (“BAFO”) to be evaluated, and a final selection will be made.
BEST AND FINAL OFFER (BAFO)

Proposals should also address the items below:
PROGRAM PLAN – 30 POINTS
• Program Plan for providing the required services is clear, professional, and rational. Must include a narrative addressing how the
organization will meet the needs and goals with respect to implementation of the scope of work detailed in Section 2. This
narrative must include a resource plan that will meet the deliverable requirements described.
• Program Plan meets the specifications and requirements of the scope of services. Must include a detailed description of
monitoring mechanisms and escalation process to ensure services are delivered according to the agreed upon scope of work and
schedule for this award.
• Demonstrates a clear understanding of the program's needs, goals, and objectives as specified. Must include a detailed
description of methods that will be used to communicate updates such as project reports, schedule changes, risks, issues, etc.,
and the frequencies with which the vendor will issue these communications to the project team.
• Must include A detailed summary describing and rationalizing the correlation between the total numbers of hours proposed for
each individual deliverable with the required work effort for that deliverable. The total number of hours discussed within the
rationalization summary per deliverable must coincide with the total number of hours proposed per deliverable in the
ATTACHMENT C – FINANCIAL SUBMISSION.
• Detail systems and structures in place to ensure project oversight and address escalations.
• Explain capacity and processes in place to collaborate effectively with the DOE.
• Program Plan is innovative and creative and exceeds the DOE’s scope as outlined.
DEMONSTRATED EFFECTIVENESS – 25 POINTS
• The evidence of prior successful experience is detailed and related to the proposed services, including effective program design,
management, and evaluation. Must include a short description of two examples of recent successfully completed projects of
similar size and scope.
• Clearly articulated details on the methods used and results obtained by those methods.
• Must include a thorough description of any experience on the part of the company or the individuals who will be managing the
project that relate to the general requirements stated in this solicitation even if not specifically for all the technologies described
in this solicitation.
• Demonstrated success in providing similar or related services in NYS or other large districts and/or related educational or
governmental or quasi-governmental organization(s).
• Demonstrated evidence of reliable quality assurance, security measures, and adherence to established timelines.
• Demonstrated ability to adapt to and accommodate shifting priorities and circumstances.
ORGANIZATIONAL CAPACITY – 15 POINTS
• A detailed description of both the project and implementation teams, including management staff responsible for ensuring
success of the project. Detail of their respective roles, responsibilities and the expertise offered by each individual must be
included. In addition, include resumes, titles, education, technical backgrounds, years of relevant experience and any professional
licenses they may hold. Indicate whether these key project personnel are subcontractors.
• Proposer must submit three (3) letters of reference or references, from organizations that have directly paid for services
rendered. Each reference must state the date(s), location(s), and description of the service(s) provided
FINANCIAL– 30% (30 Points Total)
The Proposer with the lowest overall (for the entire project) cost will receive the maximum amount of points for these criteria, with
Proposers with the second lowest cost receiving the second highest amount of points, etc.
• Use Attached Pricing Sheet, ATTACHMENT C – FINANCIAL SUBMISSION, to submit price for the services solicited. Use the yellow
cells to input price for activity/deliverable.
• The lowest overall cost offered will receive the highest score and becomes the basis for scoring all other offers proposed. This is
the formula used [(lowest overall cost/total cost submitted * total possible points for the Cost criteria = Pricing Score]
